                                          Board of Education Agenda
                                                June 10, 2025


                                PUBLIC PARTICIPATION GUIDELINES
Board of Education meetings are held in person in the board room located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael,
California. Alternatively, you can view the board meeting virtually via livestream from a computer, mobile device
or tablet at https://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting. The district has taken the following steps to assist the public in
offering public comment:

     1. In Person Public Comment. Public comment may be offered in person during the board meeting at the
        district office located at 3738 Walnut Avenue, Carmichael, California. Board Bylaw 9323 limits visitor
        comments to two (2) minutes per speaker, with no more than 30 minutes per single topic. Time will be
        extended for any speaker who uses an interpreter. Please be aware that public comments, including your
        name, become part of the public record.
     2. Online Submission of Public Comment. Members of the public may submit written comments by using the
        comment form located on the district website at http://www.sanjuan.edu/boardmeeting. If you wish to
        submit a written comment on more than one agenda item, please submit a separate form for each item on
        which you are commenting. Written comments are limited to 1,500 characters. Comments will be provided
        to the members of the board.

A.    OPEN SESSION/CALL TO ORDER/ANNOUNCEMENT OF CLOSED SESSION TOPICS - 5:30
      p.m.
      1. Visitor Comments (for closed session agenda items only)
      Board Bylaw 9323 limits visitor comments to two (2) minutes per speaker, with no more than 30 minutes per
      single topic. Time will be extended for any speaker who uses an interpreter. Public comments, including your
      name, become part of the public record.

B.    CLOSED SESSION - 5:30 p.m.
        1. Student expulsion in one case (Education Code section 48918[f]).
        2. Collective bargaining matters – discussion with negotiator Daniel Thigpen, Executive Director, Labor
           Relations and Government Affairs, regarding CSEA Chapter 127 General/Operations support,
           Chauffeurs/Teamsters Local No. 150 Transportation, Supervisors, Teachers and Certificated
           Supervisory units, and regarding non-represented groups: management and confidential units
           (Government Code section 54957.6).
        3. Personnel matters (Government Code section 54957)
           a. Superintendent’s evaluation.

C.    RECONVENE OPEN SESSION/PLEDGE OF ALLEGIANCE - 6:30 p.m.

            regular meeting, pages 2686-2689.

     Action: The administration recommends that the consent calendar, H-1 through H-9, regarding regular
     business items, be approved. Any item may be removed for further discussion and separate action following

          The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the personnel report and related items –
          appointments, leaves of absence, separations and job description/salary range change.
     2.   Purchasing Report

          The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the purchasing report - purchase orders,
          service agreements and contracts; change orders/amendments; construction and public works bids and
          contracts; and piggyback contracts.

          The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the business/financial report - notices of

          Program Self Evaluation Fiscal Year 2024-2025

     1.   Public Hearing: Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP) (Slavensky) - 7:25 p.m.

          Public Hearing/Discussion: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing

          24, 2025.

          Public Hearing/Discussion: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing

          Public Hearing/Discussion: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing
          and discuss the proposed 2025-2026 Budget, including Staffing Standards, the use of Education
          Protection Account (EPA) funds, and the substantiation of excess reserves (required by Senate Bill 858)
          as presented by the administration. Action anticipated June 24, 2025: Approval of 2024-2025 budget
          revisions, adoption of 2025-2026 budget, approval of 2025-2026 EPA expenditure plan, and approval of
          2025-2026 excess reserves.
     4.   Public Hearing No. 2: New Pacific Charter - Arden Arcade Application (Oaxaca) - 8:30 p.m.

          Public Hearing/Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing and
          approve the New Pacific Charter - Arden Arcade (NPCAA) petition for a term of five years starting July
          1, 2025, through June 30, 2030.

     5.   Public Hearing: Temporary Interfund Borrowing of Cash (Ryan) - 8:40 p.m.

          Public Hearing/Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing,
          review and adopt Resolution No. 4209 to permit temporary interfund borrowing of cash.

     6.   Presentation of Initial Proposal for the San Juan Professional Educators Coalition for 2026
          Successor Contract (Thigpen) - 8:45 p.m.

                                  NOTE: The times indicated are approximate.
The Board of Education welcomes and encourages the public’s participation at the board meetings and has devoted
time throughout the meeting for that purpose. You may comment on items included on this agenda; however, we
ask that you limit your comments to two (2) minutes, so that as many people as possible may be heard (Education
Code section 35145.5, Government Code section 54954.3).

A person with a disability may contact the Board of Education office at (916) 971-7111, or email
stephanie.cunningham@sanjuan.edu at least 48 hours before the scheduled board meeting to request receipt of an
agenda and other distributed writings in an appropriate alternative format, or to request disability-related
modifications or accommodations, including auxiliary aids or services, in order to participate in the public board
meeting.

Translation and interpretation services will be made available upon request with advance notice. If you wish to
utilize these services, please notify the Board of Education office at (916) 971-7111, or email
stephanie.cunningham@sanjuan.edu at least 24 hours before the scheduled board meeting to allow for the
scheduling of appropriate translation staff and other resources.

                                                Mission Statement
We partner with our San Juan Unified community in setting high expectations for academic achievement rooted in
equity and unity to create supportive learning experiences in which all students, families and staff can excel and
thrive.

                                    San Juan Unified Land Acknowledgement
The San Juan Unified School District acknowledges that our schools are located on the ancestral lands of the
Nisenan and Miwok peoples, who continue to care for this land as they have since time immemorial. We deeply
respect their ongoing connection to this land and recognize their resilience in preserving and revitalizing their
cultures and traditions despite historical and ongoing challenges.

We recognize the profound injustices endured by their ancestors, including genocide, forced assimilation, and
displacement, and we remain aware of the ongoing challenges and injustices they face today. As we advance in our
commitment to equity and inclusion, we pledge to strengthen our connections by fostering a culture of
understanding, respect, and active collaboration with their communities.

                                            Board of Education Minutes
                                                   May 27, 2025

Regular Meeting
Board of Education
5:30 p.m.

Open Session/Call to Order (A)
The May 27 regular meeting was called to order by the president, Ben Avey. The board meeting was held in person and
was also livestreamed on the district website.

Roll Call
Present:
Ben Avey, president
Tanya Kravchuk, vice president
Manuel Perez, clerk (left at 9:34 p.m.)
Zima Creason, member
Nick Bloise, member
Abid Stanekzai, member
Pam Costa, member

Announcement of Closed Session Topics/Closed Session Visitor Comments (A-1)
There were no closed session visitor comments.

Closed Session (B)
The meeting was then recessed with the board convening in closed session to consider a student expulsion in one case
(Education Code section 48918[f]) and to discuss one personnel matter – superintendent’s evaluation (Government Code
section 54957).

Reconvene Open Session/Pledge of Allegiance (C)
At 6:30 p.m., the May 27 regular meeting was called back to order by the president, Ben Avey. Four members of the Del
Campo High School Jr. ROTC led the group in the Pledge of Allegiance.

Minutes Approved (D)
It was moved by Ms. Creason, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, that the minutes of the May 13 regular meeting be approved.
MOTION CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY [Avey, Kravchuk, Perez, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa].

Recognition: Winston Churchill Middle School 2025 Science Olympiad (E-1)
Assistant Superintendent of Secondary Education and Programs Kristan Schnepp recognized the Winston Churchill Middle
School Science Olympiad team for their first-place finish in the Sacramento County Science Olympiad Tournament and
their ninth-place finish at the NorCal State Tournament.

Board-Appointed/District Committees (F-2)
Director of Special Education Sandra Butorac delivered a prepared report on behalf of the Community Advisory Committee
Chair Ryan Digman regarding the work of the committee.



                                                                                                                 2686
                                                                                                                         6
Closed Session/Expulsion Actions (F-5)
Mr. Perez reported that the board voted unanimously to accept a hearing panel’s recommendation of one expulsion in case
number M-24.

Visitor Comments (G)
Lisa Sotelo made comments about the Community Advisory Committee and special education.

Cerissa Brown spoke about graduation requirements and electives for special education students.

Ben commented about special education classes.

Consent Calendar Approved (H-1/H-8)
It was moved by Mr. Bloise, seconded by Mr. Perez, that the consent calendar items H-1 through H-8 be approved.
MOTION CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY [Avey, Kravchuk, Perez, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa].

Personnel (H-1)
Appointments, leaves of absence and separations – approved as submitted.

Purchasing Report (H-2)
Purchase orders, service agreements and contracts; change orders/amendments; construction and public works bids and
contracts; piggyback contracts; zero dollar contracts; and bids/RFPs – approved as submitted.

Business/Financial Report (H-3)
Notices of completion – approved as submitted.

Gifts (H-4)
Acceptance of gifts to Bella Vista High School and Laurel Ruff Transition School.

Surplus Property (H-5)
Approval to dispose of surplus property pursuant to Board Policy 3270 and Education Code sections 17545 and 17546.

Resolution No. 4205: Head Start and Early Head Start Grant Resolution 2025-2026 (H-6)
Adoption of Resolution No. 4205 approving the Head Start and Early Head Start grant resolution with Sacramento
Employment and Training Agency (SETA).

Adult Education Course Approval for 2025-2026 (H-7)
Approval of the Adult Education courses for 2025-2026.

Resolution No. 4208: Arcade Middle School Lease Amendment No. 9 (H-8)
Adoption of Resolution No. 4208, approving the ninth amendment to the lease for the Arcade Fundamental Middle School
Project No. 001-9512-P1 between San Juan Unified School District and Clark & Sullivan Builders, Inc. dba Clark/Sullivan
Construction.

Public Hearing: 2025-2026 Special Education Local Plan Area Annual Update (J-1)
Interim Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support Amy Slavensky Ph.D. presented the item and introduced
Director II of Special Education Rebecca Foster who shared information about the Special Education Local Plan Area
(SELPA) and related updates on steps currently in progress to respond to feedback and strengthen services and supports for
special education students. Mr. Avey declared the topic of the 2025-2026 SELPA annual update a public hearing and invited
the public to speak. There being no comments from the public, Mr. Avey declared the public hearing closed. Mr. Bloise
asked clarifying questions about the CAC’s involvement with the SELPA, assistive technology devices and services, and
the SELPA budget template, which Ms. Foster addressed. Mr. Bloise also inquired about the inclusion of settlement
agreements in the SELPA, which General Counsel Fhanysha Gaddis responded that no such legal obligation exists. It was
moved by Ms. Kravchuk, seconded by Mr. Perez, to approve the 2025-2026 Special Education Local Plan Area (SELPA).
MOTION CARRIED UNANIMOUSLY [Avey, Kravchuk, Perez, Creason, Bloise, Stanekzai, Costa].

District TK-12 Mathematics Update (J-2)
Dr. Slavensky introduced the topic and Director of Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation Nicole Kukral and
Professional Learning and Curriculum Innovation Program Specialist Elizabeth Julienne, who gave a presentation on the
district’s TK-12 mathematics program. Ms. Julienne shared data and spoke about the implementation and support related

                                                                                                                    2687
                                                                                                                             7
to curriculum and instruction, differentiation and intervention and professional learning. Ms. Julienne highlighted a variety
of mathematics strategies that are being implemented at various school sites across the district. Ms. Julienne also spoke
about professional learning, family engagement and community partnerships. Ms. Julienne shared the TK-8 mathematics
adoption timeline, which will be implemented in fall 2026, and she shared next steps.

Public Comment:
Juliette Porro spoke about mathematics and the Williams Act.

Board members made comments and posed questions, which staff addressed. Mr. Stanekzai inquired about strategies to
improve CAASPP results. Ms. Creason spoke about learning from others, the varying implementation strategies and
professional development options. Ms. Kravchuk discussed replicating successful practices and achieving consistency,
while also expressing support for the Building Thinking Classroom (BTC) model. Ms. Costa inquired about the expansion
of small group tutoring. Mr. Bloise asked how students with disabilities engage with the BTC model. Mr. Avey spoke about
CAASPP scores and the need for cohesive instructional practices. Staff shared information about the role of site leadership
teams, the use of standards-aligned instructional materials, and the upcoming adoption of districtwide TK-8 mathematics
curriculum. Board members also expressed interest in more frequent opportunities to engage in discussions related to
mathematics, which Superintendent Bassanelli acknowledged.

Student Discipline/Restorative Practices (J-3)
Assistant Superintendent of Educational Services Harvey Oaxaca, Ed.D., Director of Student Support Services Dominic
Covello and Multi-Tiered System of Supports Administrator on Special Assignment Katie Knifton gave a presentation
about student discipline, alternatives to suspension, restorative practices and expulsions. Dr. Oaxaca introduced the topic
and reviewed the three objectives for the 2024-25 school year. Mr. Covello discussed the outcomes of each objective,
sharing information about new state regulations and the calibration of discipline data reporting processes. Mr. Covello
presented the year-to-date data for home suspensions and in-school suspensions as well as alternatives to suspension data.
Ms. Knifton spoke about Navigate360 which provides on-demand digital lessons for K-12 students and was piloted at 29
school sites this year.

Board members made comments and posed questions, which staff addressed. Ms. Kravchuk asked clarifying questions
regarding defiance and disruptive behavior, staffing for in-school suspensions, Navigate360, and how schools reconcile
restorative practices with the consistent enforcement of behavior expectations. Ms. Kravchuk also suggested the need to
proactively educate the community about new discipline strategies to reduce confusion surrounding the significant shift in
approach. Ms. Costa inquired about the specific reading requirements of Navigate360 for elementary students and the
impact of the legislative changes on classroom dynamics and student achievement. Ms. Creason applauded the reduction
in suspensions, requested insights from school staff about the highlights and challenges of implementing the changes, and
asked if there is an intentional plan to include families in behavior discussions. Mr. Bloise inquired about the impact of
having Instructional Assistants (IAs) in classrooms. Mr. Stanekzai requested suspension data by home language, and he
advocated for presentations that promote peace and unity among students.

New Board Policy 5131.8 Mobile Communication Devices (J-4)
Chief of Staff Trent Allen presented the item for action. The proposed policy prohibits the use of smartphones or other
mobile communication devices during instructional time unless directed to do so by a staff member or are subject to the
listed exemptions.

Public Comment:
BJ Combs expressed support for a consistent, districtwide policy.

Board members made comments and posed questions, which staff addressed. Ms. Kravchuk expressed support for a
consistent districtwide policy of no cellphone use from bell to bell, with exceptions for Career Technical Education
programs and emergencies, she suggested refining the definition of mobile communication devices and she acknowledged
the potential for initial resistance but eventual normalization of a more restrictive policy. Mr. Stanekzai also expressed
support for a bell-to-bell policy. Ms. Creason expressed support for a middle-ground approach, commenting on policy
interpretation, existing school policies and the importance of community input. Mr. Perez also expressed support for a
middle ground approach, noting staffing considerations. Mr. Bloise expressed support for a bell-to-bell policy with
appropriate exceptions. Mr. Avey acknowledged both perspectives but currently supports the policy as is, commenting
about site-level implementation and future opportunities for observation and adjustments. Ms. Costa expressed support for
starting with a middle-ground approach, she spoke about staff time regarding enforcement of strict no-use rules and she
emphasized the value of input from the community and workgroups.



                                                                                                                       2688
                                                                                                                                8
Ms. Kravchuk proposed amending the third sentence of the policy to read, “Students shall not use smartphones or other
electronic devices during the school day unless directed to do so by a staff member or are subject to an exemption listed
below." It was moved by Ms. Kravchuk, seconded by Mr. Stanekzai, to adopt new board policy 5131.8 Mobile
Communication Devices as amended. MOTION FAILED 3-4 [AYES: Kravchuk, Bloise, Stanekzai; NOES: Avey, Perez,
Creason, Costa].

It was moved by Ms. Creason, seconded by Mr. Perez, to adopt new board policy 5131.8 Mobile Communication Devices
as presented. MOTION CARRIED 5-2 [AYES: Avey, Perez, Creason, Bloise, Costa; NOES: Kravchuk, Stanekzai].

Initial Proposal from California School Employees Association San Juan Chapter No. 127 to the San Juan Unified
School District for the 2024-2025 First Year Reopener General and Operations Support Units (J-5)
Acting Chief of Human Resources Daniel Thigpen presented the bargaining interests of the California School Employees
Association San Juan Chapter No. 127 for public comment. There were no requests for public comment.

Initial Proposal for the San Juan Teachers Association Successor Contract 2025-2026 (J-6)
Mr. Thigpen presented the bargaining interests of the San Juan Teachers Association for public comment. There were no
requests for public comment.

Presentation of Initial Proposal for the San Juan Professional Educators Coalition for 2026 Successor Contract (J-7)
Mr. Thigpen presented the bargaining interests of the San Juan Professional Educators Coalition for discussion pursuant to
Government Code Section 3540 et seq. and district Board Policy 4143.1. There being no questions from the board, the item
was scheduled for public comment on June 10.

Board Reports (K)
Mr. Avey reported that he attended the La Sierra High School Vietnam Veterans Memorial Day event.

Ms. Kravchuk shared that the Local Control and Accountability Plan Parent Advisory Committee (LCAP PAC) has been
experiencing challenges in achieving quorum, and she encouraged board members to check in with their appointees about
the importance of regular attendance.

Future Agenda (L)
Mr. Stanekzai suggested adding a discussion item to consider recognizing Eid as a school holiday, which was not supported
by the majority of the board.

Mr. Bloise inquired about the status of the Science of Reading resolution, which Superintendent Bassanelli advised that it
is scheduled for next fall.

Closed Session Continued (M)
At 9:34 p.m., the board returned to closed session to continue item B-2, one personnel matter – superintendent’s evaluation
(Government Code section 54957).

Adjournment (N)
At 10:38 p.m., Mr. Avey stated that there were no closed session actions to report, and there being no further business, the
regular meeting was adjourned.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                   AGENDA ITEM:                     E.1
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                   MEETING DATE:                06/10/2025

SUBJECT: Resolution Honoring and Commending Amy Slavensky, Ph.D.

DEPARTMENT: Administration

ACTION REQUESTED:
Action: The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. A-457 recognizing retiring
Interim Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support Amy Slavensky for her service and commitment to
the San Juan Unified School District.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
Amy Slavensky, Ph.D., will retire this summer after serving as San Juan Unified's Interim Deputy Superintendent
of Schools and Student Support since April 2023. This caps a long history of service in education and within San
Juan Unified where she previously served as a director of elementary and K-8 schools and the district's early
childhood education program.
ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Resolution No. A-457

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 06/02/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                Trent Allen, APR, Chief of Staff

APPROVED BY:                Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                   10
                                                        San Juan Unified School District
                                                             Resolution No. A-457

                                             Honoring and Commending Amy Slavensky, Ph.D.

WHEREAS, Amy Slavensky, Ph.D., has served the San Juan Unified School District with exemplary leadership, dedication, and unwavering
commitment in her role as Interim Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support since April of 2023, bringing to this position a rich
and extensive background including previous service as a Director of Elementary and K-8 Schools and Director of Early Childhood Education
within San Juan Unified; and

WHEREAS, Dr. Slavensky has consistently demonstrated an exceptional ability to bring diverse voices together, skillfully guiding cross-
departmental collaboration and facilitating meaningful engagement with site leaders and department leads during complex decision-making
processes, all to achieve improved and more equitable outcomes for all students; and

WHEREAS, Dr. Slavensky has been a dedicated champion for creating more equitable outcomes for students in special education, placing a
significant and impactful emphasis on actively elevating the voices of special education families and staff, and strengthening collaborative
partnerships to ensure their perspectives were integral to decision-making; and

WHEREAS, her profound kindness and care in supporting staff, her commitment to walking classrooms and visiting school sites, and her active
support for vital school initiatives such as the sponsorship teams at Katherine Johnson Middle School and Encina High School, have fostered a
positive and engaged school community; and

WHEREAS, Dr. Slavensky prioritized the professional growth of school leaders by coaching principals and focusing on the critical importance
of implementing and sustaining culturally responsive practices across all schools, thereby enhancing instructional leadership and school culture;
and

WHEREAS, Dr. Slavensky has, throughout her service, demonstrated an unwavering and deeply inspiring commitment to the students of the
San Juan Unified School District, tirelessly working to enhance their educational experiences, opportunities, and overall well-being;

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT RESOLVED, that the Board of Education of the San Juan Unified School District hereby extends its deepest
gratitude, profound respect, and sincere appreciation to Amy Slavensky, Ph.D., for her outstanding service, exemplary leadership, and significant
contributions made during her tenure as Interim Deputy Superintendent of Schools and Student Support and throughout her distinguished career
in public education.


         Attested to this
         10th day of June 2025                                                        Ben Avey, President



         ______________________________________                                       Tanya Kravchuk, Vice President
         Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools

                                    JOB DESCRIPTION
                                                                             Position Code:
                                                                     Bargaining Group: SJAA
                                                                           Salary Range: 26
                                                                          Work Calendar: 3
                                                                                  Page 1 of 3

POSITION TITLE: Director II – Early Education / Expanded Learning Programs

DEFINITION: The Director II provides strategic leadership and operational oversight of the
Early Childhood Education (ECE) and Expanded Learning Opportunities Program (ELO-P). This
role ensures program quality, alignment with district goals, and compliance with staffing,
licensing, and funding requirements.
    • Leads the integration and continuity of learning from Pre-Kindergarten PK through 3rd
        grade, supporting coherent instruction and smooth transitions.
    • Builds strong partnerships with staff, families, schools, and community organizations to
        advance program goals and equity.
    • Oversees staff, budgets, compliance, program and continuous improvement efforts
        across ECE and ELO-P. (including preschool programs such as Head Start (HS), Early
        Head Start (EHS), Infant Toddler (IT), Early Learning Academy (ELA), and before and
        after school programs).

DIRECTLY RESPONSIBLE TO: Assistant Superintendent, Elementary/K-8 Schools and
Programs

SUPERVISION OVER: ECE and Expanded Learning administrators and staff, with coordination
of assigned certificated and classified staff.

DUTIES AND RESPONSIBILTIES:

       Leadership and Program Oversight
          • Directs instructional and operational aspects of all ECE, ELO-P, and child
              development programs.
          • Coaches site leaders to align early learning practices with California’s Universal
              Prekindergarten (UPK) goals.
          • Works with school supervisors, and division leads to align program and guide
              improvement.
          • Designs and evaluates programs, including before/after school and summer
              services, ensuring equitable access.
       Vertical Alignment and Instructional Continuity
          • Ensures meaningful integration of ECE and ELO-P with site learning, Multi-Tiered
              System of Supports (MTSS), and family engagement systems by collaborating
              across departments and grade levels to support seamless PK–3 learning.
          • Aligns curriculum and assessments with state frameworks.
          • Uses student and program data (e.g., Desired Results Developmental Profile,
              reading screeners, benchmarks) to guide improvement through a continuous
              improvement process.
       Staff Management and Human Resources
          • Oversees hiring, evaluation, and development of ECE and ELO-P staff in
              partnership with assigned staff in Human Resources.
          • Addresses and resolves staff concerns and supports effective site and system-
              level operations.
          • Engages directly with families and staff when concerns arise.


                                                                                                 15
Director II – Early Education / Expanded Learning Programs
Page 2 of 3



       Compliance, Licensing, and Legal Oversight
         • Ensures compliance with federal, state, and local program regulations, including
            licensing and staffing.
         • Leads grant development, submission, and reporting aligned with state and
            federal requirements and district/community priorities.
         • Manages complaint resolution processes and ensures audit readiness.
         • Monitors and communicates policy updates, ensuring timely program
            adjustments.
       Budget and Fiscal Management
         • Develops and manages multi-source budgets, ensuring fiscal compliance and
            alignment with program goals.
         • Evaluates grant-funded efforts and identifies future funding opportunities.
       Community Engagement and Public Relations
         • Builds relationships with parents, partners, governmental and community
            agencies.
         • Communicates program goals and outcomes through outreach efforts and public
            presentations.
         • Ensures cultural responsiveness and equitable access for underserved
            communities.
       Documentation and Reporting
         • Oversees accurate and timely reporting for compliance, evaluation, and funding
            across ECE and ELO-P.
         • Aligns data systems with district departments to support audits and strategic
            planning.
         • Prepares reports as directed.

QUALIFICATIONS:

Training, Education and Experience:
Must possess an administrative services credential and have experience in working with child
development programs which include familiarity with curriculum, compliance and licensing
requirements. Three to five years increasingly responsible educational management experience
preferred. A master’s degree in education or public administration or related field from an
accredited university or college preferred.

Knowledge and Skills:
Awareness of related Education Code requirements; knowledge and skills in the administration of
child development programs; skill in communication, both oral and written; skill in interpersonal
communications with students, parents, staff and administration.

Physical Characteristics: (Consideration will be given to reasonable accommodation)
Sufficient vision to read printed materials; sufficient hearing to conduct in person and telephone
conversations; sufficient physical mobility to move about the district and drive a car; ability to
speak in an understandable voice with sufficient volume to be heard in normal conversational
distance, on the telephone, and in addressing groups; physical, mental and emotional stamina to
endure long hours under sometimes stressful conditions.




                                                                                                     16
Director II – Early Education / Expanded Learning Programs
Page 3 of 3

 Other Characteristics:
 Possession of a valid California driver's license; willing to work additional hours periodically;
 willing to travel locally.

WORKING CONDITIONS:

 Work Environment:
 • School campus and district office environment
 • Moderate noise
 • Frequent interruptions and significant distractions

 Other Characteristics:
 • Ability to work additional hours periodically and weekends on occasion
 • Ability to travel locally and attend conferences/seminars periodically

 This job description is not a complete statement of essential functions and responsibilities. The
 district retains the discretion to add or change typical duties of a position at any time.

POSITION TITLE: Coordinator, Technology Support
Program Manager Desktop Support

DEFINITION: Under general direction, Contributes to the leadership and direction of district -
wide technology planning, technology acquisition and application development; coordinates,
facilitates, and provides general support in the purchase and installation of hardware and
software; assists with the establishment and enforcement of technology standards and security
policies for hardware and software; provides training in the use of hardware and software.
Ensures maximum utilization of staff, resources, and equipment to meet the district’s
technology deployment and support demands.

DIRECTLY RESPONSIBLE TO: Program Manager, Instructional Technology Senior Director
of Technology Services

SUPERVISION OVER: Classified staff as assigned to Technology Services Help Desk and
Desktop Support

DUTIES AND RESPONSIBILTIES: (Any one position may not include all of the duties listed;
the listed examples do not include all duties and responsibilities which may be found in positions
within this classification.)
• Participates in the leadership and direction of technology planning, technology acquisition
    and application development.
• Assists with the establishment of district technology security policies and standards.
• Coordinates all school district-wide technology hardware and software support and
    repair issues.
• Develops and implements installation, support and repair procedures for technology
    at schools and district sites.district-wide
• Consults with school site administrators and staff regarding all school-based hardware and
    software installation, maintenance, support, and repair issues.
• Develops and implements a training program for site-based classified staff who provide
    technology support at schools and for district departments.
• Co-facilitates principal school technology and support group meetings as needed.
• Coordinates technology support and information meetings with school site and district staff
    as needed.
• Develops and coordinates educational hardware and software training classes for school and
    department district staff as needed.
• Supervises, coordinates, and evaluates the performance of assigned staff.
• Meets with appropriate hardware and software vendors.
• Coordinates hardware/software needs and prioritizes with appropriate district instructional
    staff. them for use by the Senior Director of Technology Services.
• Identifies and reviews staffing needs.
• Assists in the hiring of all technology services hardware/software support staff under their
    supervision.
• Maintains district hardware/software web-based reference pages regarding standardized
    equipment list.

                                                                                                     18
Program Manager Desktop Support
Page 2 of 3

   •   Reviews all hardware and software requests for the district.
   •   Assists with departmental budget and monitoring of expenditures in
       accordance with established policies
   •   Performs other duties as assigned.

   QUALIFICATIONS:

   Education and Experience:
       Any combination of education and experience equivalent to a bachelor’s degree in
       education, computer science, information technology, or related field; and progressively

       responsible duties including developing technology support programs for schools, training
       of teachers and support staff in the use hardware and application software, and
       troubleshooting hardware and software problems.

   Licenses and Certifications:
   • Valid California Class C Driver’s License issued by the California Department of Motor
       Vehicles within 30 days of hire or before driving any vehicle requiring the license.
   Knowledge, Skills, and Abilities:
   • Knowledge of school technology needs.
   • Knowledge of micro-based software applications.
   • Knowledge of microcomputer hardware.
   • Knowledge of school instructional technology needs.
   • Knowledge of troubleshooting procedures for microcomputer hardware as well as for Mac
      and Windows platforms.
   • Ability to develop and implement district-wide school technology support programs.
   • Ability to effectively facilitate meetings.
   • Ability to plan, organize, and conduct in-service training programs to support and integrate
      educational technology in instruction.
   • Ability to communicate effectively both orally and in writing.
   • Ability to establish and maintain effective relationships with those contacted in the course
      of work.
   • Ability to select, assign, motivate, direct, and evaluate staff.
   • Ability to understand basic LAN issues.
   • Ability to maintain consistent, punctual and regular attendance.

   Health:
   • Evidence of freedom from active tuberculosis (CA Education Code section 49406)


   WORKING CONDITIONS:

   Work Environment:
   • Indoor office environment
   • School classroom environment
   • Continuous contact with staff
   • Drive a vehicle to conduct work

   Typical Physical Characteristics: (with or without the use of aids; consideration will be given to
   reasonable accommodation)
   • Inspect documents and other written materials with fine print.
   • Communicate to exchange information in person, with groups and on the telephone.                   19
Program Manager Desktop Support
Page 3 of 3
   •   Move about school sites and district departments to conduct work.
   •   Remain in a stationary position for extended periods of time.
   •   Operate tools and equipment requiring repetitive hand movement and fine coordination.
   •   Physical, mental and emotional stamina to endure long hours under sometimes stressful
       conditions.

   This job description is not a complete statement of essential functions and responsibilities. The
   district retains the discretion to add or change typical duties of a position at any time.

longer needed by the district. The superintendent or designee shall arrange for the sale or disposal of district
personal property in accordance with board policy and the requirements or state law.

The superintendent or designee shall identify to the board all items not needed by the district together with their
estimated value and a recommended disposition.

BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                        MEETING DATE:               06/10/2025

SUBJECT: California State Preschool Program (CSPP) and General Child Care and Development Program (CCTR)
Program Self Evaluation Fiscal Year 2024-2025

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board approve the California State Preschool Programs (CSPP) and
General Child Care and Development Program (CCTR), Program Self-Evaluation for Fiscal Year 2024-2025.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The California Department of Education and California Department of Social Services in accordance with
California Code of Regulations, Title 5 CCR, Section 18279(b)(3), must provide an assessment, in narrative
format, summarizing the San Juan Unified School board members participation in the Program Self-Evaluation
process. California State Preschool Program (CSPP) and General Child Care Development Program (CCTR)
contractors are required to analyze findings from the self-evaluation and develop both a written list of tasks needed
to modify the program for all areas needing improvement and procedures for ongoing monitoring to assure that
areas continue to meet contract terms and conditions.

For Fiscal Year (FY) 2024–2025, all CSPP and CCTR contractors will use the Early Education Division’s (EED)
Program Instrument (PI) to conduct the FY 2024-2025 Program Self-Evaluation (PSE) and are required to
complete a survey to satisfy the submission requirements for the PSE. The survey will identify how programs met
or did not meet the contract terms and conditions.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Program Self Evaluation - California Department of Social Services
2. Program Self Evaluation - California Department of Education

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
N/A

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 06/02/2025

  CCD 01: Program Integrity: Family Data Files

  Eligibility

  Family Fees

  12. Agency has completed a file review from each contract type held. If any of the files reviewed were missing
  eligibility or need criteria, the program shall include within their program action plan steps to adjust practices to
  ensure all documentation collected meets eligibility and need requirements.
  Has the contractor met this requirement?

  No

  13. Describe a plan to resolve and meet this requirement.
  During the file review the following was found: NOA wasn't sent within 30 days, the paystub should have been
  calculated using 24 pay periods versus 26, rental agreement doesn't match the SMUD bill, a child was counted in the
  family size but was over 18 and out of high school, pay was different on the employment verification versus the pay
  stubs, and certification ended on February 2025 and there is no reminder in the file to recertify. To resolve the findings,
  we will meet with the enrollment team monthly to review any new requirements.


  14. Describe the agency’s internal procedures to review family data files continuously to ensure all
  documentation needed to certify families for services is collected and verified.

  The enrollment team will be asked to increase family data checks to ensure all required documents and verification of
  need are completed. A yearly review of files will be done by the ECE Support Specialist.

6. Dimension II. Family Engagement

  18. Families with children enrolled in the programs are selected according to the priorities of that program.
  (Select all that apply)

  Contractor maintains a waiting list or central eligibility list by contract type
  Families are enrolled according to the priorities of the program
  Written information for families includes the priorities for the program and describes how family selection occurs
  CCD 07: Parent Involvement and Education [Applies to CMIG, CFCC, CHAN, CCTR / Does not apply to CAPP,
  C2AP, C3AP, and CMAP]

  There is a parent involvement and education component, including the sharing of program goals and structure
  with families. Describe how the program promotes family engagement through the following:

  19. Parent Education and Involvement: Describe the education and involvement opportunities for parents.
  Responses shall be inclusive of Parent Advisory Committee activities, parent education opportunities, parent
  orientation, parent conferences and family engagement activities.
  Our program focuses on offering a variety of opportunities for parent engagement throughout the year. Some highlights
  include: four parent advisory meetings during the year; annual parent orientation with each family before their child's first
  day; parent conferences offered twice a year; a resource packet given to each family at the beginning of the year; the
  continued use of parent surveys; and home language support via program staff, translation technology, and community
  support. At our parent advisory meetings, we have brought in guest speakers to go over strategies that parents can use
  with their children called, Setting Limits, and our district's FACE (Family and Child Engagement) department. These
  events are in addition to our first meeting where we went over enrollment in the district, including the different options
  available to families; and our second meeting where we focused on our Nutrition Program, including the meals the
  children receive during the day, available waivers, and how we meet each child's nutritional needs. We encourage
  parent participation in our programs through our open door policy and volunteering in the classroom.
7. Dimension II. Family Engagement

  20. Describe the process used for identification of any health and social services needed by the family or for the
  child(ren). Include in the response the process for referring and conducting follow-up to ensure families were
  connected to the requested support and/or resources.

  Our program requires parents to attend a health and social service screening. For health services, the parents complete
  health screeners, and our nurses follow up as needed. For mental health, parents are referred to our mental health
  specialist (MHS). For social services, a family partnership agreement is filled out, and the family meets with the school
  community worker (SCW) numerous times throughout the year to receive assistance with resources and services. Our
  families have access to our nurses, school community workers, and mental health specialist throughout the year. We
  also connect families with our district's family engagement department for any additional services they may need.
8. Dimension III. Program Quality

  21. Written information for families: Program has provided written policies to child’s parents or legal guardians
  of the limitations on disenrollment, including suspension and expulsion. This notification shall be in writing and
  shall inform parents on how they may file an appeal, to the department, in the event of the suspension or
  expulsion of a child. Has the contractor met this requirement
  No

                                                                                                                                  33
  22. Describe a plan to resolve and meet this requirement.

  The administration and support staff will create a clear, step-by-step process that outlines all needed interventions and
  documentation needed for a student with behavioral needs. This process will then be reviewed with all staff in the
  program and parents at orientation, including (but not limited to) limitations on disenrollment, including suspension and
  expulsion. The process will include how to file an appeal directly to CDSS in the event of an expulsion or suspension.
  The handbook will include the policies and procedures.

  23. Describe the local contacts or partners who will provide consultation, social service and/or mental health, or
  behavioral services for the program:

  In our school district, the Family & Engagement department and school community workers work with families to provide
  social services resources. Families are connected with the mental health specialist for mental health issues and the
  school nurse for health problems. These services are accessible to families throughout the school year.
  24. Program has documentation of policies and procedures outlining suspension and expulsion and as to which
  behaviors would warrant an expulsion that meet the requirements outlined in the Child Care Bulletins for
  Expulsion And Suspension Procedures In Child Care And Development Programs

  Has the contractor met this requirement?
  Yes

  25. Describe the training and resources provided to children, families, and providers (if applicable) to support
  the social emotional development of children in the program and inclusive practices used to help children
  exhibiting serious and persistent challenging behaviors.

  We are able to identify children's social needs through the Ages and Stages Questionnaire, Family Partnership
  Agreement, and screenings. We provide referrals to the appropriate agencies for services and follow up to ensure
  identified needs have been met. An Inclusion Specialist, School Psychologist, and Mental Health Therapist assist
  teachers and parents with children exhibiting serious and persistent challenging behaviors.

13. Dimension III. Program Quality

  31. DRDP Online upload is complete for each rating period.
  Has the contractor met this requirement?
  No
  32. Describe a plan to resolve and meet this requirement.

  For our Infant/ Toddler programs we are meeting this standard and will continue to meet this standard through regularly
  established professional development sessions and reminders of when assessments are due. In School-age, we are
  following our Continuous Improvement Plan to be in compliance with completing DRDPs for each rating period. The
  Education Content has met with the school-age staff to go over state-funded requirements, the use of DRDP for school-
  age children, the DRDP online assessment platform, best practices for developmentally appropriate activities and how
  lesson/activity planning supports teachers with rating students. Additional refresher training and support will be provided
  to all classroom staff as needed.
  33. Program has completed Summary of Findings on all Classroom(s)/Home(s).
  Has the contractor met this requirement?
  No

  34. Describe a plan to resolve and meet this requirement.
  Filling out and updating the Summary of Findings forms will be incorporated into staff development meetings to ensure a
  higher rate of completion and to offer support to individual classroom staff as needed. Form submission will be tracked
  by support staff, and administrators will be notified if any are missing after the twice-yearly submission deadlines.

  35. Describe the program’s process for providing developmentally, linguistically, and culturally appropriate
  activities to children in the program that aligns with individual and classroom/home DRDP data.

  Using the updated California Learning Foundations as a guide, classroom staff will be given additional professional
  development on integrating DRDP data with learning goals and lesson plans to support individual skill development,
  with an emphasis on linguistic and cultural sensitivity toward all families.

  • For infant/toddler, the new Infant/Toddler Learning and Development Foundations (ITLDF) will be shared with
  classroom staff, and professional development will be offered that focuses on each domain in turn.
  • For school-age the Preschool/Transitional Kindergarten Learning Foundations (PTKLF) will be shared with classroom
  staff, and professional development will be offered that focuses on each domain in turn.
  • Best practices professional development will be provided to reinforce flexible screen usage as one tool in a variety of
  strategies, rather than the only available option.

  39. What percentage of your qualified teachers hold Associate Teacher Permits?
  Applies to CCTR, CMIG, and CHAN / Does not apply to CFCC


  100%

  40. Describe agency practices and procedures to recruit and retain qualified staff.
  Applies to CCTR, CMIG, and CHAN / Does not apply to CFCC

  CCTR infant-toddler classrooms have met all the required recruitment of qualified staff. We offer competitive salaries/
  benefits, and trainings for staff. CCTR school-age has struggled with the requirement of qualified staff. All of our
  teachers are permitted. However, our assistants do not all meet the qualifications of an associates teacher's permit. To
  be in compliance, we have only used substitutes with the appropriate units. We continue to work with Human
  Resources to do hiring events, continual advertisements of the open positions, and competitive salaries/benefits.

15. Dimension III. Program Quality

  41. Describe some of the staff development opportunities provided to staff/providers. Include the process for
  using data to identify the training opportunities provided to staff and/or providers. In the response include how
  Program Self-Evaluation and other data sources are used to determine the training needs of staff and/or
  providers.

  CCTR IT, Staff Wellness/Managing Stress, DRDP Training, CACFP Training, Lesson Planning, Safe Environment
  Training, and Parent Meeting and Resources are all a part of the training opportunities for staff. Existing professional
  development structures will be made available to CCTR staff to the extent possible, with an initial focus on social-
  emotional skill development and supporting dual language learners.

  Staff Development Program (for CCTR SA), we will have SACERS, CACFP, and social and emotional training for all
  staff. School-age CCTR staff will receive more direct professional development and coaching from program support
  staff to ensure understanding and completion of DRDP assessments , lesson plans, and individualization plans.

  Staff are surveyed annually and more often by our Leadership team, if needed, to identify any developmental topics,
  workshops, and/or trainings they feel would benefit their position. The Leadership team reviews the topics submitted by
  staff including any findings from professional development surveys, assessments, grant mandates and/or reviews for
  that school year to identify the training and developmental needs of the staff for the following year.

  42. Program has a process for orienting new staff. Documentation is maintained on the training and resources
  provided to new staff and/or providers.

  Yes

17. Dimension IV. Administrative

  46. Describe two goals for your program. Include in your response; the data collected to identify the goal, and
  the action steps to be taken to improve the practice or program requirement.

  1st goal: DRDP's, Individualized Development Plans(IDP's), and lesson plans were collected. It was concluded that the
  results from the documentation and/or ratings for DRDP measures was not completed timely or fully, due to a variety of
  reasons, including staff shortages and workload issues. In addition, program-wide DRDP data indicated that more
  social-emotional, language and literacy, and dual language learner support is needed across classrooms and age
  levels.
  To address these concerns over the course of two to three school years:
  · Additional materials for curricula (cognitive and SEL) will be ordered and integrated into the available materials for
  classroom staff, and refresher training on the curriculum materials will be offered.
  · Identified classroom staff will be offered specific coaching and small group professional development as needed on
  program expectations regarding assessments.
  · Classroom staff will be offered professional development designed to support their growth in understanding the
  Desired Results system, including how to create learning goals, lesson plans and daily schedules that support individual
  skill development, using existing program curricula and supplementing as necessary with additional tools.
  · Additional resources will be gathered and shared to support all classroom staff learning and growth in the areas of
  social-emotional, language and literacy, and dual language learner support.

  2nd goal: Assisting teachers with challenging behaviors. We'll do this by creating specific systems of observation, which
  will not only help teachers identify behaviors but also show them what to do next. We'll be implementing integration of
  services and the Alternative Formal Observation tool to streamline this process.



  47. Parent Survey: Describe the results of the parent survey and the action plans to address the feedback
  received from parents.
  For IT-Toddler, the feedback was positive. The parents would like us to be open from 8-5. Unfortunately, this is not
  currently an option.
  School age: Issues with consistent staffing and parent/teacher communication. Staff will receive the results from the
  parent surveys, as well as feedback on what parents feel is lacking in our program. A recurring theme was that parents
  were unsure what their children were doing throughout the day. We will be working with staff on developing newsletters
  to address this issue including visuals on the parents' board. Work will be done with the staff to include more in depth
  newsletters, posting of the classroom's daily schedules and lesson plans, and guidelines for developing communication
  skills with parents.
  48. Briefly describe how staff and board members were a part of the self-evaluation process.

  Staff is part of the PSE through a process we call triangulation. During a staff meeting, we go over the data from the
  ITERS/SACERS, parent surveys, and the fall/spring DRDP's. After we have gone over the data, we formulate a plan of
  how we can provide support and meet our program goals for our families, students, and staff for the 25-26 school year.
  After the PSE is written, we send it to the School Board for approval and input.
18. Dimension IV. Administrative

  49. Program has completed the Agency Summary of Findings.
  Has the contractor met this requirement?

  No

  50. Describe a plan to resolve and meet this requirement.
  Filling out and updating the Summary of Findings forms will be incorporated into staff development meetings to ensure a
  higher rate of completion and to offer support to individual classroom staff as needed. Form submission will be tracked
  by support staff, and administrators will be notified if any are missing after the twice-yearly submission deadlines.




                                                                                                                              37
  51. List key findings from the agency summary of findings and the action steps that will be taken to address the
  findings. If the program provides services to multiple age groups provide one key finding with action steps for
  each age group served.
  Infant-Toddler: Positive
  •Over 80% of children were at Exploring Later or Building Earlier for ATL -REG 4: Curiosity and Initiative in Learning,
  LLD 2: Responsiveness to Language and PD-HLTH 1: Perceptual-Motor Skills and Movement Concepts.
  •We will continue to offer training on toddler development, learning, and activities, in addition, to Teaching Pyramid for
  Infants and Toddlers to support reflective practice for teachers.

  Infant-Toddler: What we are working on:
  •Reg-5 measures showed less than 5% growth in the Building Earlier level of development from Fall 2024 to Spring
  2025.
  ATL-REG 2: Self-Comforting
  ATL-REG 5: Self Control of Feelings and Behavior
  COG 8: Cause and Effect
  COG 9: Inquiry through Observation and Investigation and
  COG 11: Knowledge of the Natural World

  How we will work on it:
  •Support staff will analyze and reflect on the data trends to see what factors may be affecting a slower than expected
  level of growth.
  •Classroom staff will be trained/refreshed on specific curricular strategies, including parallel play, promoting social play
  activities, and extended imaginative play.
  •Classroom staff will be given additional training and support on how to efficiently capture notes and other
  documentation of children's progress, and how to extend learning activities in authentic, engaging, and inquiry-based
  ways.

  School-age: Positive
  •Teachers were trained/refreshed on the DRDP assessment tool for school age, with an emphasis on understanding the
  descriptors for all measures, as well as clear and efficient note taking practices to capture evidence for the
  assessments.
  •Teachers were trained on the DRDP Online data portal website, and given resources for further study in advance of the
  portal being used for all school age students in the upcoming school year.

  How we will continue:
  •Classroom staff will have additional training and support opportunities as they shift to using the DRDP Online tools in
  the 2025-26 school year.
  •Classroom staff will be offered information and training on the new DRDP 2025 assessment tool, and the option to
  become early adopters of the tool.

  What needs work:
  •Not all subsidized children were assessed on the DRDP school age tool this year.
  •Not all children were assessed using the same database/method (some on paper, some in a database) making it
  difficult to gather and analyze the resulting data.
  •Regular monitoring for DRDP completion was inconsistent.

  How we will work on it:
  •Support staff will implement a plan to address the lack of completed assessments, and will monitor completion more
  consistently by having all data accessible through the DRDP Online web portal.
  •Support staff will increase their own level of knowledge about the features and affordances of DRDP Online and DRDP
  2025 to better support classroom staff.




19. Dimension IV. Administrative

  52. Program has completed Agency Summary of Findings.
  Has the contractor met this requirement?

  Yes



                                                                                                                                 38
  53. Using the Agency Summary of Findings describe two key findings from the ERS and what action steps the
  program is taking to address the key findings. Include resources, training, or materials to address the key
  findings. If the program provides services to multiple age groups provide one key finding with action steps for
  each age group served.

  ITERS data indicated that more training and support is needed in the subscales of health practices and
  diapering/toileting. The subscales supervision and evaluation of staff and opportunities for professional growth indicate
  areas of the program that would benefit from additional evaluation and development of program processes for staff
  support.
  To address these concerns over the course of two to three school years:
  •Classroom staff will be offered ongoing professional development throughout the school year with the health team and
  related content specialist designed enhance their knowledge of required health practices.
  •Focused in-person training of diapering and toileting procedures will be done with classroom staff, along with continued
  observation by support staff to evaluate the effectiveness of current procedures and adjust steps as needed to support
  staff efficacy and meet health standards.
  •Optional focus groups will be organized to evaluate the staff support subscales to determine the changes needed to
  provide growth in the areas of supervision, evaluation, and opportunities for professional growth.
  •Data will be collected from the staff identifying procedures that would support them in ongoing professional growth,
  principally in the area of onboarding for new staff.

  SACERS data indicated that support is needed in the subscales of health practices and music and movement. The
  subscales supervision and evaluation of staff and opportunities for professional growth indicate areas of the program
  that would benefit from additional evaluation and development of program processes for staff support.
  Subscale #12 Health Practices
  1.1, 3.1There should be a designated area set aside for a sick /contagious child or a plan to effectively separate a sick
  child from the rest of the children to minimize contamination until the child can be picked up.
  Action: The requirement will be reviewed during opening PD, in New Teacher training, and as needed throughout the
  year. Part of the training will include where to locate this information. Support staff will complete self-assessment checks
  at a minimum of 2X a year to determine if this requirement is in place. Support will be provided as needed.
  Subscale #20 Music and movement
  5.1, 5.2 Various types of music and movement activities and experiences should be made accessible for free choice.
  This could include Cd's, and musical instruments incorporating different languages, genres, and cultures.
  Action: Resources will be provided at Professional Development trainings to display ideas and resources available to
  bring various types of music and instruments into the classroom and make it available for free choice. Support staff will
  complete self-assessment checks at a minimum of 2X a year to determine if this requirement is in place. Support will be
  provided as needed.

24. Optional: Celebrating Promising Practices

  CCDD has implemented a process to identify exemplary work and practices during the contract monitoring
  review process. A promising practice is defined as a practice, procedure, or activity that exceeds program
  requirements. The CCDD wants to celebrate continuous program improvement successes with our contractors.
  Programs have the option of sharing a promising practice which may include a narrative explaining the
  exemplary work done by the contractor and may include uploading a picture, sharing data outcomes, video
  links, etc.



  60. Share your promising practices: Narrative
  Our GUFF event, Grandparents, Uncles, Fathers and Friends, at Marshall was fantastic! A huge shout out to our School
  Community Workers and Christina for doing such a stellar job organizing and celebrating the male role models in our
  families!




  61. Upload your files here, if applicable.

  guff_picture.jpg
25. Review

  Please review your responses and click the "Submit" button once you
  have confirmed your responses. If you wish to keep a copy of your survey,
  scroll down to the bottom of the page and click "Download PDF Version."
26. Thank You!

  Thank you for completing the Program Self-Evaluation Survey for Child Care and Development Programs at the
  California Department of Social Services (CDSS).
  A copy of your responses will be sent to rhoppe@sanjuan.edu (if the email does not arrive after a couple
  minutes, please check your Spam or Junk Mail folder). A copy of your responses was also sent to the CDSS.

  (A copy will also be sent to Tracey Maupin-Uppal and Elyse Polis)

  Password to open the attachment: FY24-25PSE

League representatives for the 2025-2026 school year as specified in the attached list.

the power to vote on issues that concern athletics at the league, sectional, and state levels. It is recommended that
the board approve the high school principals and their designees, as listed in the attachment.

    RESOLUTION BEFORE THE SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
 BOARD OF EDUCATION APPROVING LEASE AMENDMENT NO. 6 FOR THE
MARIEMONT ELEMENTARY SCHOOL MODERNIZATION & INTERIM HOUSING
         PROJECT SJUSD PROJECT #130-9495-P1 & 130-9495-P2

                                     RESOLUTION NO. 4120

WHEREAS, sections 17406 of the Education Code authorize school districts, including the San
Juan Unified School District (“District”), to use the lease-leaseback procurement process; and

WHEREAS, the District Board of Education (“Board”) previously approved the award of the
Site Lease and Facilities Lease to S+B James Construction California, Inc. for this Project, which
approval for the Facilities Lease per resolution 4062; and

WHEREAS, Section 4.4.2.4.5 of the Facilities Lease Agreement states all remaining funds shall
be returned to the district by deduction outlined in Amendment #6;

WHEREAS, Section 4.4.2.4.1 Construction Contingency, Section 4.4.2.4.3 Owner Contingency,
and Exhibit H – Preconstruction Services will be reduced and return all unused funds to the
district.; and

WHEREAS, Exhibit C - Lease Payment Schedule is revised to reflect the returning of unused
funds;

NOW, THEREFORE, the San Juan Unified School District Board of Education does hereby
resolve as follows:

Section 1. The foregoing recitals are hereby adopted as true and correct.

Section 2. The Board approves the revised length of the term as outlined in Amendment #6, and
approves the revised Lease Payment Schedule as reflected in Exhibit C.

Section 3. The Board approves the revised Facilities Lease Agreement amount as outlined in
Amendment #6.

      Effective June 10, 2025, the Facilities Lease Agreement dated January 24, 2023 between the San
      Juan Unified School District and S+B James Construction California Inc. for the Mariemont ES
      Modernization/Interim Housing is amended to reconcile pre-construction services, the total
      base rent to include used contingencies, and lease payment schedules for the Modernization
      and Interim Housing as follows:

1. Section 4.4.2 Total Base Rent for the Project is amended to be Fifteen million Eight hundred six
   thousand Two hundred fifty-seven dollars ($15,806,257.00) broken down by Modernization –
   $14,421,095 + Interim Housing – $1,385,162.
2. Section 4.4.2.4.1 Construction Contingency is amended to be the final amount after reconciliation of
   executed contingency draw authorizations of Four hundred five thousand Four hundred eighty-one
   dollars ($405,481.00) broken down by Modernization – $405,481.00 + Interim Housing – $0.
3. Section 4.4.2.4.3 District Contingency is amended to be the final amount after reconciliation of executed
   contingency draw authorizations of One million One hundred forty-one thousand One hundred
   seventy-four dollars ($1,141,174.00) broken down by Modernization – $1,067,423.00 + Interim
   Housing – $73,751.00.
4. Exhibit C – Lease Payment Schedules are amended for both the Modernization and Interim Housing.
5. Exhibit H – Preconstruction Services is amended to be the final amount after reconciliation of used site
   investigation allowances of Four hundred sixty-three thousand Seven hundred ninety dollars
   ($463,790.00) broken down by Modernization $455,017.00 + Interim Housing $8,773.00.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                   AGENDA ITEM:                     H.8
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                   MEETING DATE:                06/10/2025

SUBJECT: Public Safety Cell Tower Project at Rio Americano High School

DEPARTMENT: Operations

ACTION REQUESTED:
The superintendent is recommending that the board adopt Resolution No. 4212, giving authority to enter into a
ground lease agreement with Public Safety Towers, LLC.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
Public Safety Towers, LLC (“PSTC”) provides cellular services to first responders that enables them to more
effectively respond to emergencies such as fires. In exchange for rent and other considerations, the District is
interested in leasing space to PSTC so that they can construct a cell tower at Rio Americano High School. This
tower would greatly enable first responders to react to and address evolving fire danger and other circumstances
providing a benefit to the community.

On May 15 at 6:00 p.m., a virtual community meeting was held in partnership with Public Safety Towers
Company to provide an opportunity for early input from neighbors and community members. The presentation and
recording of the community meeting are available for viewing here: www.sanjuan.edu/riocelltower.

ATTACHMENT(S):
1. Summary of Outreach
2. Resolution No. 4212
3. Cell Tower Lease Agreement

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
Facilities Committee: 04/01/2025, 05/06/2025

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent's Cabinet: 06/02/2025

FISCAL IMPACT:
N/A

PREPARED BY:                Frank Camarda, Chief Operations Officer

APPROVED BY:                Melissa Bassanelli, Superintendent of Schools




                                                                                                                   67
                                        Attachment 1



Community Outreach Efforts for Proposed Cell Tower at Rio Americano High School

To ensure transparency and gather community input on the proposed cell tower at Rio
Americano High School, the district undertook a multi-pronged outreach effort:

School and Neighborhood Communication: In April, notification letters were mailed to nearby
residents, and Principal Cliff Kelly proactively shared information about the proposal with Rio
Americano students, families and staff. Communication included information about a virtual
meeting to learn more and ask questions. Information about the community meeting was also
added to the Rio Americano website calendar and news section.

Virtual Community Meeting: On May 15, the district hosted a virtual informational meeting in
partnership with Public Safety Towers Company to present the proposal and collect feedback
from the community. Two community members attended the meeting.

Online Information Hub: A dedicated webpage was launched to provide ongoing updates and
resources about the proposed project at www.sanjuan.edu/riocelltower. The webpage contains
a copy of the presentation from the community meeting, as well as a recording of the meeting.

Facilities Committee Review: The proposal was presented to the district’s Facilities
Committee on April 1, where it received unanimous support. An update was provided at the
committee’s May 6 meeting, including details about the upcoming virtual meeting and a copy of
the draft lease agreement for review and discussion.

                         BEFORE THE BOARD OF TRUSTEES
                    OF THE SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT
                        SACRAMENTO COUNTY, CALIFORNIA
                              RESOLUTION NO. 4212

                   RESOLUTION APPROVING LEASE AGREEMENT

       WHEREAS, the San Juan Unified School District (“District”) is the owner of certain real
property known as Rio Americano High School located at 4540 American River Drive,
Sacramento CA 95864, and more specifically depicted in Exhibit “A,” attached hereto and
incorporated herein by reference (the “Property”); and

       WHEREAS, Public Safety Towers, LLC (“PSTC”) provides cellular services to first
responders that enables them to more effectively respond to emergencies such as fire; and

       WHEREAS, PSTC is looking for additional space to construct and operate a cell tower
to address existing coverage gaps in their network. Expanding such coverage would allow them
to more efficiently provide services to first responders; and

       WHEREAS, PSTC identified the District’s Property as an optimal space for such a tower
and accordingly approached the District to gauge the District's interest in an agreement; and

        WHEREAS, the District desires to lease space to PSTC for the purposes described
above. The District and PSTC negotiated the terms of the lease agreement and determined that
the District would lease a portion of the Property to PSTC in exchange for monthly rent and
other monetary consideration; and

        WHEREAS, it is in the best interests of the District to lease the Property to PSTC, and
the District is therefore willing to lease the Property to PSTC under the terms and conditions of
that certain lease agreement, a copy of which is attached hereto as Exhibit “B” and incorporated
herein by reference.

                      ****************************************

NOW, THEREFORE, BE IT FOUND, DETERMINED AND RESOLVED BY THE
BOARD OF EDUCATION OF THE SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT,
SACRAMENTO COUNTY, CALIFORNIA, AS FOLLOWS:

       1.      The foregoing recitals are hereby adopted as true and correct.

       2.      The Board of Trustees hereby approves and ratifies that certain lease agreement
               with the Public Safety Towers, LLC. for the lease of space at the District’s Rio
               Americano High School property. Said lease agreement, in substantially the form
               submitted to this Board and attached to this Resolution as Exhibit “B,” shall be
               executed on behalf of the District by the District’s Superintendent, or his/her




                                                                                                    69
              designee, subject to such changes or revisions therein as the Superintendent may
              find necessary or desirable.

       3.     The District’s Superintendent, or his/her designee, is authorized and directed to
              take such other and further actions as may be necessary or convenient to carry out
              the purpose and intent of this Resolution.

       4.     This Resolution shall take effect immediately upon its adoption.

The foregoing Resolution was adopted at a meeting of the Governing Board of the San Juan
Unified School District on June 10, 2025, by the following vote:
