3.   RESTRICTED CASH AND CASH EQUIVALENTS

     Cash and equivalents as of June 30, 2024, consist of the following:

                    Cash and equivalents with County Treasury                  $   22,641,874
                    Cash and equivalents with fiscal agent                         76,552,114
                           Total cash and equivalents                          $   99,193,988

     Cash and equivalents in County Treasury – The Measure P Building Fund maintains cash in the
     Sacramento County Treasury (the Treasury). The Treasury pools funds with those of other districts
     in the county and invests the cash. The share of each fund in the pooled cash account is separately
     accounted for and interest earned is apportioned quarterly based on the relationship of a fund’s
     daily balance to the total of pooled cash and investments.

     Participants’ equity in the Treasury investment pool is determined by the dollar amount of the
     participant deposits, adjusted for withdrawals and distributed income.

     The Treasury is authorized to deposit cash and invest excess funds by California Government Code
     Section 53648 et seq. and is restricted by Government Code Section 53635, pursuant to
     Section 53601.

     Cash and equivalents with fiscal agent – Cash and equivalents with fiscal agent are held by the
     Treasury and represent bond proceeds restricted for capital projects.

     Derivative investments – The Measure P Building Fund did not directly enter into any derivative
     investments. Information regarding the amount invested in derivatives by the Treasury was not
     available.

     Interest rate risk – Interest rate risk is the risk that changes in market interest rates will adversely
     affect the fair value of an investment. Generally, the longer the maturity of an investment, the
     greater the sensitivity of its fair value to changes in market interest rates. As of June 30, 2024,
     weighted average maturity of the investments contained in the Treasury is approximately 274 days.

     Credit risk – Generally, credit risk is the risk that an issuer of an investment will not fulfill its
     obligation to the holder of the investment. This is measured by the assignment of a rating by a
     nationally recognized statistical rating organization. The Treasury investment pool does not have a
     rating provided by a nationally recognized statistical rating organization.

     Custodial credit risk – Custodial credit risk for deposits is the risk that, in the event of the failure
     of a depository financial institution, a government will not be able to recover its deposits or will not
     be able to recover collateral securities that are in the possession of an outside party. The California
     Education Code and the District’s investment policy do not contain legal or policy requirements
     that would limit the exposure to custodial credit risk for deposits other than the following provision
     for deposits: the California Government Code requires that a financial institution secure deposits
     that are made by state or local governmental units by pledging securities in an undivided collateral
     pool held by a depository regulated under state law (unless so waived by the governmental unit).
     The market value of the pledged securities in the collateral pool must equal at least 110% of the
     total amounts deposited by the public agencies.

4.   INTERFUND TRANSACTIONS WITH OTHER DISTRICT FUNDS

     Transfers to and from other District Funds are operating transfers from a fund receiving revenue to
     a fund through which the resources are being expended. Transfers in from other District Funds were
     as follows for the year ended June 30, 2024:

         The District’s General Fund transferred $1,000,000 to the Measure P Building Fund as a
         portion of Sacramento County Office of Education’s AB 179 funds.

         The District’s County School Facilities Fund transferred $319,428 to the Measure P Building
         Fund for Office of Public School Construction reimbursements.


5.   COMMITMENTS AND CONTINGENCIES

     The District is subject to legal proceedings and claims which arise in the ordinary course of
     business. In the opinion of management, the amount of the ultimate liability with respect to these
     actions will not materially affect the financial position or results of operations of the Measure P
     Building Fund.

     As of June 30, 2024 the Measure P Building Fund has approximately $80 million in outstanding
     commitments and construction contracts.

 INDEPENDENT AUDITOR’S REPORT ON INTERNAL CONTROL
OVER FINANCIAL REPORTING AND OTHER MATTERS BASED ON
   AN AUDIT OF FINANCIAL STATEMENTS PERFORMED IN
   ACCORDANCE WITH GOVERNMENT AUDITING STANDARDS



Independent Bond Citizens’ Oversight Committee
  and Board of Education
San Juan Unified School District
Carmichael, California


We have audited, in accordance with the auditing standards generally accepted in the United States of
America and the standards applicable to financial audits contained in Government Auditing Standards
issued by the Comptroller General of the United States, the financial statements of the Measure P
Building Fund of the San Juan Unified School District (the District), which comprise the balance sheet as
of June 30, 2024; the statement of revenues, expenditures, and change in fund balance; and the related
notes to the financial statements, and have issued our report thereon dated April 21, 2025.

Report on Internal Control over Financial Reporting

In planning and performing our audit of the financial statements, we considered the District’s internal
control over financial reporting (internal control) as a basis for designing audit procedures that are
appropriate in the circumstances for the purpose of expressing our opinions on the financial statements,
but not for the purpose of expressing an opinion on the effectiveness of the District’s internal control.
Accordingly, we do not express an opinion on the effectiveness of the District’s internal control.

A deficiency in internal control exists when the design or operation of a control does not allow
management or employees, in the normal course of performing their assigned functions, to prevent, or
detect and correct, misstatements, on a timely basis. A material weakness is a deficiency, or a
combination of deficiencies, in internal control, such that there is a reasonable possibility that a material
misstatement of the entity’s financial statements will not be prevented, or detected and corrected, on a
timely basis. A significant deficiency is a deficiency, or a combination of deficiencies, in internal control
that is less severe than a material weakness, yet important enough to merit attention by those charged with
governance.

Our consideration of internal control was for the limited purpose described in the first paragraph of this
section and was not designed to identify all deficiencies in internal control that might be material
weaknesses or significant deficiencies and therefore, material weaknesses or significant deficiencies may
exist that were not identified. Given these limitations, during our audit we did not identify any
deficiencies in internal control that we consider to be material weaknesses. We identified a certain
deficiency in internal control, described in the accompanying schedule of findings and questioned costs
as item 2024-001 that we consider to be a significant deficiency.

Report on Compliance and Other Matters

As part of obtaining reasonable assurance about whether the District’s financial statements are free from
material misstatement, we performed tests of its compliance with certain provisions of laws, regulations,
contracts, and grant agreements, noncompliance with which could have a direct and material effect on the
financial statements. However, providing an opinion on compliance with those provisions was not an
objective of our audit, and accordingly, we do not express such an opinion. The results of our tests
disclosed no instances of noncompliance or other matters that are required to be reported under
Government Auditing Standards.

District’s Response to Findings

Government Auditing Standards requires the auditor to perform limited procedures on the District’s
response to the findings identified in our audit and described in the accompanying schedule of findings
and questioned costs. The District’s response was not subjected to the other auditing procedures applied
in the audit of the consolidated financial statements and, accordingly, we express no opinion on the
response.

Purpose of This Report

The purpose of this report is solely to describe the scope of our testing of internal control and compliance
and the results of that testing, and not to provide an opinion on the effectiveness of the organization’s
internal control or on compliance. This report is an integral part of an audit performed in accordance with
Government Auditing Standards in considering the District’s internal control and compliance.
Accordingly, this communication is not suitable for any other purpose.

                   SCHEDULE OF FINDINGS AND RECOMMENDATIONS
                                   JUNE 30, 2024



FINANCIAL STATEMENT FINDINGS

2024-001 – SIGNIFICANT DEFICIENCY OVER BOND ISSUANCE

   Criteria:

   An essential aspect of any organization’s internal control over its financial reporting process is the
   ability to produce accurate financial data in accordance with generally accepted accounting
   principles (GAAP). Accurate financial reporting enables management and the Independent Citizens’
   Bond Oversight Committee and Board of Education to have a sound financial basis for decision-
   making.

   Condition:

   The Measure P Building Fund is used to account for the receipt and expenditure of proceeds from the
   sale of bonds. The major expenditures of the Measure P Building Fund are related to the purchase
   and improvement of sites and the construction and modification of buildings in accordance with
   approved projects. The District’s Bond Interest and Redemption Fund is used to account for deposits
   of bond premiums and the repayment of bonds. Errors in the recording of debt transactions in the
   Measure P Building Fund affecting the balances as of June 30, 2024, were detected during the audit.
   This identified error resulted in adjustments to the revenues and expenditures of the Measure P
   Building Fund.

   Questioned Costs:

   None.

   Cause:

   The District’s review process did not identify errors in the accounting for bond issuance transactions
   in accordance with GAAP and the California School Accounting Manual.

   Effect:

   Revenues and expenditures for the Measure P Building Fund were reduced by $122,135,563 to
   properly reflect the recording of debt issuance premiums and repayment of bonds in accordance with
   GAAP.

   Recommendation:

   We recommend that District management develop a process to ensure sufficient review is performed
   during the financial close process to identify significant errors in the fund financials. Debt
   transactions that are infrequent in nature, such as debt issuances, should be reviewed against bond
   documents and County administered accounts to ensure all related transactions have been recorded
   accurately in accordance with GAAP.

   Views of Responsible Officials and Planned Corrective Actions:

   The District has developed a plan for verifying the funds used when booking debt to ensure the
   appropriate fund is identified.

                          INDEPENDENT AUDITOR’S REPORT



Board of Education and Independent Citizens’ Bond
 Oversight Committee for Measure P
San Juan Unified School District
Carmichael, California


We have conducted a performance audit of the San Juan Unified School District (the District) Measure P
General Obligation Bonds for the year ended June 30, 2024.

We conducted our performance audit in accordance with Government Auditing Standards issued by the
Comptroller General of the United States. Those standards require that we plan and perform the audit to
obtain sufficient, appropriate evidence to provide a reasonable basis for our conclusion based on our audit
objectives. We believe that the evidence obtained provides a reasonable basis for the findings and
conclusions based on our audit objectives.

Our audit was limited to the objectives listed on page 2 of this report which includes determining
compliance with the performance requirements for the Proposition 39 and Measure P General Obligation
Bonds under the applicable provisions of Section 1(b)(3)(C) of Article XIIIA of the California
Constitution and Proposition 39 as they apply to the bonds and the net proceeds thereof. Management is
responsible for the District’s compliance with those requirements.

Solely to assist us in planning and performing our performance audit, we obtained an understanding of the
internal controls of the District to determine if internal controls were adequate to help ensure the District’s
compliance with the requirements of Proposition 39, as specified by Section 1(b)(3)(C) of Article XIIIA
of the California Constitution. Accordingly, we do not express any assurance on the internal controls.

The results of our tests indicated that, in all significant respects, the San Juan Unified School District
expended Measure P General Obligation Bond funds for the year ended June 30, 2024, only for the
specific projects developed by the District’s Board of Trustees and approved by the voters, in accordance
with the requirements of Proposition 39, as specified by Section 1(b)(3)(C) of Article XIIIA of the
California Constitution.

OBJECTIVES

The objectives of our performance audit were to document that the San Juan Unified School District (the
District) expended Measure P General Obligation Bonds (the Bonds) funds for the year ended June 30,
2024 only for purposes approved by voters and only for specific projects developed by the District’s
Board of Trustees, in accordance with the requirements of Proposition 39, as specified by
Section 1(b)(3)C of Article XIIIA of the California Constitution; note any incongruities or system
weaknesses; and provide recommendations for improvements.


SCOPE OF THE AUDIT

The scope of our performance audit covered the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024. Expenditures incurred
after the issuance of the Bonds and prior to July 1, 2023, were covered in a previous examination. The
expenditures included all account and project codes associated with the Bond projects. Expenditures
incurred subsequent to June 30, 2024 were not reviewed or included within the scope of our audit.


BACKGROUND INFORMATION

On November 8, 2016, the electorate of the San Juan Unified School District approved the $750 million
Measure P general obligation bonds with greater than 55% of the votes in favor.

On March 7, 2017, the District issued General Obligation Bonds Election of 2016 (Series 2017 Bonds) in
the amount of $39,920,000 to finance specific construction, acquisition, and modernization projects.
These bonds matured during succeeding years through August 2019 and were issued at interest rates
ranging from 2.00% to 2.50%.

On January 22, 2019, the District issued General Obligation Bonds of Election 2016 (Series 2019 Bonds)
in the amount of $80,000,000 to finance specific construction, acquisition, and modernization projects.
These bonds mature during succeeding years through August 2029 and were issued at 4.00% interest rate.

On October 29, 2020, the District issued General Obligation Bonds, Election 2016, Series 2020 in the
amount of $150,000,000. The bonds range in maturity date with final payoff on August 1, 2041 and
interest rates ranging from 0.25% to 4.00%.

On April 21, 2022, the District issued General Obligation Bonds, Election 2016, Series 2022 in the
amount of $150,000,000. The bonds range in maturity date with final payoff of August 1, 2046 and
interest rates ranging from 2.18% to 5.00%.

On January 18, 2024, the District issued General Obligation Bonds Election of 2016, Series 2024 in the
amount of $125,000,000, for the purpose of financing the renovation, construction and improvement of
school facilities. The bonds mature during succeeding years through August 2049. The bonds were issued
at interest rates ranging from 4% to 5%.

As of June 30, 2024, total outstanding debt related to Measure P is $412,310,000.

The financial activity related to the Measure P General Obligation Bonds is recorded in sub-Fund 26
(Measure P Building Fund) of Fund 21 (Building Fund) in the District's audited financial statements for
the year ended June 30, 2024.


PROCEDURES PERFORMED

We obtained the Measure P Building Fund detailed general ledger for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024.
We obtained the actual invoices and other supporting documentation for a sample of expenditures to
ensure compliance with Proposition 39 and Measure P General Obligation Bond funding. We performed
the following procedures:

   We verified the mathematical accuracy of the expenditures included in the Measure P Building Fund
    detailed general ledger for the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024.

   Solely to assist us in planning and performing our performance audit, we obtained an understanding
    of the internal controls of the District to determine if internal controls are adequate to help ensure the
    District’s compliance with the requirements of Proposition 39.

   We determined that the Independent Citizens’ Bond Oversight Committee is fulfilling its duties as
    required by Education Code 15278 and as outlined in their Committee Bylaws.

   We selected a sample of expenditures in the fiscal year ended June 30, 2024. The sample was selected
    to provide a representation across construction projects, vendors and expenditure amounts. For each
    selection, we performed the following procedures:

    1. Reviewed supporting documentation to ensure that funds were properly expended on the specific
       projects outlined on the publicized list and met the requirements for bidding, if applicable.

    2. Verified the expenditure was for construction, rehabilitation, or replacement of school facilities,
       including furnishing and equipping of District facilities, deferred maintenance upgrades or the
       acquisition or lease of real property facilities and verified that funding was not used for salaries of
       school administrators or other operating expenditures of the District.

The District incurred total expenditures of $139,873,200 through June 30, 2024, for the Measure P
projects listed in the following Schedule of Activities.

            AND MEASURE P GENERAL OBLIGATION BONDS
                                      PERFORMANCE AUDIT
                                          JUNE 30, 2024



CONCLUSION

Based upon the procedures performed, we found that, for the items tested, the San Juan Unified School
District has properly accounted for the expenditures of the Measure P General Obligation Bonds. Further,
it was noted that the funds were not used for salaries of school administrators or other operating
expenditures. Our audit does not provide a legal determination on the District’s compliance with specific
requirements.


MANAGEMENT COMMENTS AND RECOMMENDATIONS

None.

      Communications With Those Charged With Governance

      We have audited the financial statements of the Measure J, N, and P Building Funds of the San Juan
      Unified School District (the District) for the year ended June 30, 2024. Professional standards require
      that we provide you with information related to our audits. We are providing the District’s Bond Oversight
      Committee (BOC) and the Board of Education (Board) with information regarding the scope and results
      of the audits to assist the BOC and the Board in overseeing management’s financial reporting and
      disclosure process. This information is intended solely for the use of the BOC, Board, and
      management of the District and is not intended to be, and should not be, used by anyone other than
      these specified parties. The following pages summarize these required communications.

      opinions about whether the financial statements prepared by                     and do not purport to, and do not present fairly the financial position
      management with your oversight are fairly presented, in all material            of the District as of June 30, 2024, or the changes in its financial
      respects, in conformity with U.S. generally accepted accounting                 position for the year then ended in conformity with accounting
      principles. Our audit of the financial statements does not relieve you          principles generally accepted in the United States of America. Our
      or management of your responsibilities.                                         opinions were not modified with respect to this matter.

      As part of our audit, we considered the internal control of the District.

                                            INTERACTIONS WITH MANAGEMENT
      Planned Scope and Timing of the Audit                                           We performed the audits according to the planned scope and timing
                                                                                      previously communicated in our engagement contract.


      Management        Consultations        with     Other     Independent           To our knowledge, there were no such consultations with other
      Accountants                                                                     accountants.
      In some cases, management may decide to consult with other
      accountants about auditing and accounting matters, similar to
      obtaining a “second opinion” on certain situations. If a consultation
      involves application of an accounting principle to the District’s
      financial statements or a determination of the type of auditor’s
      opinion that may be expressed on those statements, our professional
      standards require the consulting accountant to check with us to
      determine that the consultant has all the relevant facts.


      Disagreements with Management                                                   We are pleased to report that no such disagreements arose during
                                                                                      the course of our audits.
      For purposes of this letter, professional standards define a
      disagreement with management as a financial accounting, reporting,
      or auditing matter, whether or not resolved to our satisfaction, that
      could be significant to the financial statements or the auditor’s report.


      Management Representations                                                      We requested certain representations from management in a letter
                                                                                      to us.

SAN JUAN UNIFIED SCHOOL DISTRICT                                    AGENDA ITEM:                     J.1
BOARD OF EDUCATION

                                                                    MEETING DATE:                06/10/2025

SUBJECT: Public Hearing: Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP) (Slavensky) - 7:25 p.m.

DEPARTMENT: Teaching and Learning

ACTION REQUESTED:
Public Hearing/Discussion: The superintendent is recommending that the board hold a public hearing regarding
San Juan Unified’s Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP). Action anticipated: June 24, 2025.

RATIONALE/BACKGROUND:
The 2024–2027 Local Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP) is a three-year plan designed to support the
continuous improvement of student outcomes across the eight state priority areas, with a particular focus on
English learner, low-income, and foster youth students. The LCAP is reviewed and updated annually based on
multiple data sources, including input from educational partners, state and local data, and relevant research. In
accordance with California Education Code, the governing board must hold a public hearing prior to adoption to
solicit feedback and recommendations on proposed actions and expenditures. Additionally, both the LCAP and the
district budget must be approved by the board at the same meeting.
ATTACHMENT(S):
1. 2025-26 LCAP Presentation
2. 2025-26 LCAP Draft
3. 2025-26 LCAP Infographic Overview
4. Educational Partner Themes
5. Superintendent Responses to Comments from the LCAP PAC and DELAC
6. 2025-26 Draft LCAP Public Comment

BOARD COMMITTEE ACTION/COMMENT:
LCAP Parent Advisory Committee: 09/12/2024, 10/17/2024, 03/20/2025, 05/22/2025
Curriculum, Standards, Instructional and Student Services Committee: 10/02/2024
District English Learner Advisory Committee: 10/24/2024
Superintendent’s Parent Advisory Committee: 01/23/2025
Superintendent’s Student Advisory Council: 10/01/2024
Facilities Committee: 10/01/2024

PREVIOUS STAFF/BOARD ACTION:
Superintendent’s Cabinet: 10/21/2024, 11/04/2024, 12/02/2024, 01/21/2025, 02/03/2025, 03/24/2025, 04/7/2025,
04/21/2025, 06/02/2025
FISCAL IMPACT:

                         LCAP GOALS
     1. Student Achievement and Implementation of State Standards -
     Improve achievement and outcomes for all students as measured by
     performance on statewide assessments, English proficiency, and college
     and career preparedness.


     2. Student Engagement and Course Access - Provide students with
     engaging programs, course work, and opportunities that address
     attendance, dropout, graduation, and access to a broad course of study.



     3. School Conditions, Climate, and Family Engagement - Address factors
     both inside and outside the classroom that impact student success such
     as family engagement, health, safety, discipline, connectedness, facilities,
     materials, and staffing.


     4-11. Schools with Non-Stability Rates of +25% and Socioeconomically
     Disadvantaged Rates of +70% - Within three years, improve outcomes
     for student groups that have the lowest performance level on one or
     more state indicators, and address any underlying issues with
     credentialing, subject matter preparation, and retention of the school’s
     educators.

                                                                                                                                                           Attachment 1




                          Language and Cultural Connections
                            • Promote inclusive practices in physical spaces, food options, curriculum, and professional learning.
                            • Provide additional bilingual instructional support, language programs, and translation services.
                            • Adapt communication styles and scheduling to be culturally responsive.
                            • Offer clubs, student organizations, and cultural events to foster belonging and connectedness.

                          Academic Support Including College/Career Readiness
                            • Expand tutoring, credit recovery, and specialized support, especially for English learners and students with disabilities.
                            • Increase communication about available academic resources and services.


      KEY
                            • Upgrade technology, classroom furniture, and reduce student-teacher ratios.
                            • Provide earlier, consistent guidance on college and vocational pathways


     THEMES               Safety and Behavior Support
                            • Increase supervision and implement clear behavior protocols with restorative practices.
                            • Maintain restrooms and monitor to reduce vaping and address substance use.
                            • Enhance student safety before and after school, especially in parking lots.

                          Mental and Physical Health
                            • Expand access to mental health services by hiring more practitioners.
                            • Provide additional support for English learners, low-income students, foster youth, and homeless youth with unique
                             challenges.
                            • Create wellness spaces and offer support services for families.
                            • Offer healthier cafeteria options and improve physical spaces to promote a healthy school environment.

                          Engagement Opportunities
                            • Use multilingual, accessible communication channels for families.
                            • Provide clear information on expectations, events, and resources.
                            • Host more resource fairs, workshops, and parent education events.
                            • Increase student and family voice in decision-making and extracurricular activities.

School districts receive funding from different sources: state funds under the Local Control Funding Formula
(LCFF), other state funds, local funds, and federal funds. LCFF funds include a base level of funding for all LEAs
and extra funding - called "supplemental and concentration" grants - to LEAs based on the enrollment of high
needs students (foster youth, English learners, and low-income students).

District is $682,002,915, of which $508,694,744 is Local Control Funding Formula (LCFF), $118,487,129 is other
state funds, $19,676,506 is local funds, and $35,144,536 is federal funds. Of the $508,694,744 in LCFF Funds,
$75,108,473 is generated based on the enrollment of high needs students (foster youth, English learner, and
low-income students).

                               LCFF Budget Overview for Parents


The LCFF gives school districts more flexibility in deciding how to use state funds. In exchange, school districts
must work with parents, educators, students, and the community to develop a Local Control and Accountability
Plan (LCAP) that shows how they will use these funds to serve students.

This chart provides a quick summary of how much San Juan Unified School District plans to spend for 2025-26. It
                 shows how much of the total is tied to planned actions and services in the LCAP.

The text description of the above chart is as follows: San Juan Unified School District plans to spend
$750,049,547 for the 2025-26 school year. Of that amount, $122,293,403 is tied to actions/services in the LCAP
and $627,756,144 is not included in the LCAP. The budgeted expenditures that are not included in the LCAP will
be used for the following:


The budgeted expenditures that are not included in the LCAP will be used for the following:
     • District and school general operating and housekeeping expenses
     • Routine repair and maintenance
     • Insurance
     • Transportation
     • Instructional materials and resources
     • Special education
     • Other certificated and classified employee salaries and benefits
These expenditures, while not included in the Local Control and Accountability Plan, are integral to the district's
ability to serve students and families.

The text description of the above chart is as follows: In 2024-25, San Juan Unified School District's LCAP
budgeted $74,903,555 for planned actions to increase or improve services for high needs students. San Juan
Unified School District actually spent $66,698,574 for actions to increase or improve services for high needs
students in 2024-25.


The difference between the budgeted and actual expenditures of $8,204,981 had the following impact on San
Juan Unified School District’s ability to increase or improve services for high needs students:

Actual 2024-25 expenditures are preliminary. The final numbers for the year will be available in August 2025.
Looking across goals 1-3, the difference between our planned expenditures and preliminary actual expenditures
was due to staffing and substitute shortages, and shifts in funding to sources outside of the LCAP. The impact of
the difference in funding resulted in the inability to fully implement some actions as planned. Additionally, the
impact of the difference in funding results in carryover funding for the upcoming 2025-26 school year.

Plan Summary [2025-26]
General Information
A description of the LEA, its schools, and its students in grades transitional kindergarten–12, as applicable to the LEA. LEAs may also provide
information about their strategic plan, vision, etc.
SJUSD has 65 schools, employs approximately 5,500 staff, covers 75 square miles, and serves the communities of Arden-Arcade,
Carmichael, Citrus Heights, Fair Oaks, Gold River, and Orangevale. Within the district are diverse groups of students including a significant
number of refugee and immigrant students who speak Spanish, Farsi, Pashto, Russian, Arabic, Ukrainian, and many other languages.

Approximately 63.8% of our students are unduplicated low income, English learner, and foster youth and are enrolled at every school
campus throughout the district. San Juan Unified works in partnership with students, families, staff, and community members to design and
provide challenging and comprehensive standards-based academic programs and services that include:
    • Montessori, Spanish Dual Immersion, Rapid Learner, International Baccalaureate, CIVITAS, career technical pathways,
       Advancement Via Individual Determination (AVID), and other specialties
    • Infant/toddler, preschool, and adult education
    • Differentiated academic, behavioral, and social-emotional supports through a Multi-Tiered System of Support (MTSS)
    • Enrichment opportunities in areas such as science, technology, engineering, arts, and mathematics (STEAM)

Schools with socioeconomically disadvantaged rates of +70% and non-stability rates of +25% (Non-Stability Rate = Percent of students who
do not complete 180 instructional days at one school) receive Equity Multiplier funding. These schools include Cameron Ranch Elementary,
Dyer-Kelly Elementary, Encina High, Greer Elementary, Howe Avenue Elementary, Katherine Johnson Middle, San Juan High, and Starr
King K-8.

2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                      Page 4 of 213
                                                                                                                                                  261
The Learning Recovery Emergency Block Grant (LREBG) was established in response to the emergency caused by the COVID-19 pandemic
to assist schools serving pupils in the long-term recovery from the COVID-19 pandemic, including addressing pupil learning, mental health,
and overall well-being. San Juan Unified School District has no unexpended LREBG funds to report.

As of October 2, 2024 (CALPADS Census Day), there are approximately 39,376 TK-12 students enrolled. Our diverse student population
includes socioeconomically disadvantaged (SED) youth - 58.4%, English learners (EL) - 22.6%, students with disabilities (SWD) - 14.1%,
homeless youth (HM) - 4.6%, and foster youth (FY) - 0.3%. Our ELs speak the following languages: Spanish - 22.7%, Farsi (Persian) -
26.1%, Pashto - 17.0%, Russian - 13.2%, Arabic - 3.6%, Ukrainian - 5.5%, Filipino or Tagalong - 0.4%, and other non-English - 21.0%. By
ethnicity, San Juan students identify as White - 45.2%, Hispanic - 26.0%, Asian - 12.9%, African American - 5.8%, Filipino - 0.8%, Pacific
Islander - 0.7%, American Indian or Alaskan Native - 0.4%, and two or more races - 8.1%.




Reflections: Annual Performance
A reflection on annual performance based on a review of the California School Dashboard (Dashboard) and local data.
Successes, challenges, and actions were identified in San Juan Unified’s annual performance review of the 2024 Dashboard.

Successes
   • Increased English Language Arts (ELA) proficiency for the following student groups: Filipino and foster youth.
   • Increased math proficiency for the following student groups: Filipino, foster youth, homeless, and socioeconomically disadvantaged.
   • Chronic absenteeism rates reduced overall, especially for the following student groups: African American, American Indian/Alaskan
      Native, Hispanic, Homeless, socioeconomically disadvantaged, students with disabilities, two or more races, and white.
   • Suspension rates declined for the following student groups: foster youth, Pacific Islander, students with disabilities and two or more
      races.
   • College and career readiness rates increased overall, especially for student groups: Filipino, foster youth, Hispanic, homeless,
      socioeconomically disadvantaged, and White.
   • Increased graduation rates for the following student groups: English learners and Filipino student groups.

Challenges
   • Decreased ELA proficiency for the following student groups: American Indian/Alaskan Native, Asian, English Learner, Hispanic,
       Long-Term English Learner, Pacific Islander and White.
   • Decreased math proficiency for the following student groups: American Indian/Alaskan Native, Asian, English Learner, Hispanic,
       Long-Term English Learner, and White.
   • Decreased English Learner progress towards English language proficiency.
   • Decreased English Learner reclassification rate to Fluent English Proficient.
   • Increased chronic absenteeism rate for foster youth students.
   • Suspension rates increased for the following student groups: American Indian/Alaskan Native, Asian, English Learner, Hispanic,
       homeless, Long-Term English Learner, and White.
   • Decreased college and career readiness rate for African American, Asian, and Two or More Races.
2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                Page 5 of 214
                                                                                                                                            261
     • Decreased graduation rates for the following student groups: African American, foster youth, homeless, students with disabilities,
       Two or More Races, and White.
     • Lowest Performing Student Groups by California School Dashboard Indicator:
African American: English Language Arts, Mathematics, Suspension
American Indian or Alaska Native American: Mathematics, Suspension
English Learner: English Language Arts, Mathematics
Foster Youth: Graduation, Chronic Absenteeism
Homeless: English Language Arts, Graduation, Suspension
Long Term English Learner: English Language Arts, Mathematics, Suspension
Students with Disabilities: English Language Arts, Mathematics, Graduation, College/Career

As required for the three-year LCAP, SJUSD continues to address the challenges of student groups and schools identified by the 2023 and
2024 Dashboard by using a continuous improvement process that includes:
    • Building on the work we have done to maintain continuity of learning.
    • Analyzing multiple sources of data that include educational partner input, state and local data, practitioner experience, and research
       to identify the unique needs of our students.
    • Adjusting existing and creating new actions to address the unique needs of our students.

Examples of actions in the LCAP that address the unique needs of our student groups in the identified Dashboard indicators include:

English Language Arts:
   • Explicit foundational skills instruction and intervention to strengthen existing literacy programs.
   • Integrated English language development (ELD) professional learning focused on addressing the needs of Long-Term English
       Learners.
   • Building disciplinary language, reading, and writing skills aligned to the ELA and ELD standards.
   • Early identification of students at risk or in need of additional assessment and intervention in reading.

Mathematics:
   • Professional learning opportunities focused on evidence based early numeracy instruction.
   • Summer learning opportunities for students to accelerate growth towards math standards.
   • Additional middle and high school math sections to provide differentiated instruction in smaller groups.

English Learner Progress:
   • Offering research-based instructional strategies to accelerate English language acquisition while teaching academic content.
   • Targeted listening, speaking, reading, and writing support for English learners aligned to the domains assessed on the ELPAC.
   • Explicit language instruction through integrated and designated ELD.

Chronic Absenteeism:
   • Expanding the Attendance Academy at elementary and middle schools to provide targeted support services.
   • Expanding transportation for homeless and foster youth students.

2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                 Page 6 of 215
                                                                                                                                             261
     • Supporting families and students who were chronically absent in the prior year with district resources.
     • Supporting schools with analyzing attendance metrics, creating site attendance improvement plans, and implementing attendance
        interventions based on a tiered model.
     • Increasing the number of site habitual truancy and chronic absenteeism conferences, and district Student Attendance Review Board
        meetings.

Suspension Rates:
   • Providing training on neuroscience and evidence-based practices to assist with creating warm and welcoming environments.
   • Implementing effective teaming practices.
   • Implementing interventions and alternative practices to suspension.

Graduation and College/Career Readiness:
   • Mentoring focused on providing wraparound services.
   • Counseling and support in developing 4-year graduation plans using Naviance.
   • Credit recovery options such as Apex, extended year intersession, and independent study.
   • Special outreach, recruitment, and opportunities for priority student groups such as Advancement Via Individual Determination
      (AVID) and Equal Opportunity Schools (EOS).


Reflections: Technical Assistance
As applicable, a summary of the work underway as part of technical assistance.
Differentiated Assistance (DA) is provided to assist districts in improving student performance for student groups with the lowest performance
levels in two or more Local Control Funding Formula (LCFF) state priority areas and related Dashboard indicators. San Juan Unified met the
criteria for Differentiated Assistance in 2024 for the following five student groups:
     • African American: ELA, math, suspension
     • American Indian/Alaskan Native: Math, suspension
     • Homeless: ELA, suspension, graduation
     • Long Term English Learners: ELA, math, suspension
     • Students with Disabilities: ELA, math, graduation, college/career

We continue to partner with and receive ongoing support from the Sacramento County Office of Education focused on improving outcomes
for student groups with the lowest performance levels on the CA School Dashboard indicators. Supports include monthly meetings focused
on continuous improvement practices as well as SCOE led professional learning communities.




2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                 Page 7 of 216

Comprehensive Support and Improvement
An LEA with a school or schools eligible for comprehensive support and improvement must respond to the following prompts.
Schools Identified
A list of the schools in the LEA that are eligible for comprehensive support and improvement.
La Entrada Continuation High

Support for Identified Schools
A description of how the LEA has or will support its eligible schools in developing comprehensive support and improvement plans.
San Juan Unified School District provides schools that meet the criteria for Comprehensive Support and Improvement (CSI) the opportunity
to participate in a Networked Improvement Community (NIC). The NIC is a five-part series that works through the four stages of continuous
improvement which includes conducting a needs assessment and root cause analysis, defining goals and evidence-based interventions,
prototyping and implementing solutions, and measuring and reporting on the impact of the improvement plans. The purpose is for schools to
make changes in practice that reflect student needs and result in improved outcomes, experiences, and opportunities for students.

Each of the five network meetings will provide school leadership teams with an opportunity to engage in the continuous improvement process
with a focus on CA School Dashboard Indicators in need of improvement, and the learning space to collaborate with partner school sites
within the network to share ideas, resources, and best practices.

In addition to the network meetings, a coach is assigned to CSI schools to provide expert guidance and personalized support throughout the
NIC process. The coach will also act as a thought partner during the network meetings to help eligible CSI schools achieve their goals, and to
ensure that school leadership teams are equipped with the necessary tools and resources to drive improvement. Collectively, the NIC and
coach will support the development and implementation of the School Plan for Student Achievement (SPSA) in alignment with the Local
Control and Accountability Plan (LCAP).


Monitoring and Evaluating Effectiveness
A description of how the LEA will monitor and evaluate the plan to support student and school improvement.
The district NIC facilitator and coach will engage in the following practices to monitor and evaluate the plan to support student and school
improvement: 1) provide regular check-ins with site administrators and school improvement teams to refine and modify improvement plans
based on regular review of data and information; 2) review each school’s Plan-Do-Study-Act (PDSA) plans and inquiry cycles to identify
measurable and actionable goals; and 3) create and share site reports with each school to provide feedback and monitor progress of plans.




2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                 Page 8 of 217
                                                                                                                                             261
Engaging Educational Partners
A summary of the process used to engage educational partners in the development of the LCAP.

School districts and county offices of education must, at a minimum, consult with teachers, principals, administrators, other school personnel,
local bargaining units, parents, and students in the development of the LCAP.

Charter schools must, at a minimum, consult with teachers, principals, administrators, other school personnel, parents, and students in the
development of the LCAP.

An LEA receiving Equity Multiplier funds must also consult with educational partners at schools generating Equity Multiplier funds in the
development of the LCAP, specifically, in the development of the required focus goal for each applicable school.

LCAP PRESENTATIONS:                                                                  On May 22, 2025, a draft plan of the LCAP was presented to the
   • San Juan Unified School District Board of Education                             Local Control and Accountability Parent Advisory Committee (LCAP
   • Local Control and Accountability Plan Parent Advisory                           PAC) and the District English Learner Advisory Committee (DELAC)
      Committee (LCAP PAC)                                                           for in separate meetings. Both groups reviewed the draft LCAP and
   • District English Learner Advisory Committee (DELAC)                             provided comments to the Superintendent. The Superintendent’s
                                                                                     responses were posted on the district LCAP website prior to the
                                                                                     public hearing and were shared with the LCAP PAC and the DELAC.

                                                                                     The draft LCAP was posted on the district LCAP website on May 22,
                                                                                     2025 to allow for public review and comment prior to the public
                                                                                     hearing on June 10, 2025. On June 24, 2025, the LCAP and district
                                                                                     budget were adopted by the Board of Education, and the Local
                                                                                     Indicator results were reported.



A description of how the adopted LCAP was influenced by the feedback provided by educational partners.
District focus teams and the Special Education Local Plan Area (SELPA) team reviewed and monitored 2024-25 LCAP actions, metrics, and
expenditures throughout the year. Additionally, LCAP PAC perspectives and insights, educational partner key themes, and climate survey
results were used to support the planning and development of the 2025-26 LCAP. Please see goals 1-11 for specific actions, metrics, and
expenditures that were informed by educational partner feedback.




2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                             Page 13 of 222
                                                                                                                                                          261
Goals and Actions
Goal
  Goal #     Description                                                                                    Type of Goal
    1        Student Achievement and Implementation of State Standards - Improve achievement and outcomes Broad Goal
             for all students as measured by performance on statewide assessments, English proficiency, and
             college and career preparedness.
State Priorities addressed by this goal.
 XPriority 2: State Standards (Conditions of Learning)
 XPriority 4: Pupil Achievement (Pupil Outcomes)
 XPriority 8: Other Pupil Outcomes (Pupil Outcomes)

An explanation of why the LEA has developed this goal.
A review of Dashboard data results showed:
    • African American, English learner, and homeless student groups received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard
       English language arts and mathematics indicators.
    • Students with Disabilities student group received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard college/career indicator.
    • 29 out of 65 schools had one or more lowest performance levels on a 2023 Dashboard state indicator (see addendum A)
    • 53 out of 65 schools had one or more student groups that received the lowest performance level on a 2023 Dashboard state
       indicator (see addendum B).

During the 2021-24 LCAP cycle, educational partners indicated that:
    • LCAP goals were too broad and difficult to measure using data.
    • Including current year and previous year data in LCAP was confusing.
    • Not all required student groups were reported in the LCAP.

State and local data and educational partner input revealed a need to increase:
    • Alignment between goals, metrics, and actions.
    • Clarity to the public and board about our goals, and how we are measuring and monitoring progress.
    • Focus on English learner, low income, foster youth, and student groups with the lowest performance level on a Dashboard indicator.

As a result, the 2024-27 LCAP:
    • Goals are aligned to the state priority areas and required LCAP metrics.
    • Metrics include required state and local data that reflects the most recent full-year data as well as all education code required
       student groups in the reporting of data.


2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                  Page 14 of 223
                                                                                                                                               261
     •   Actions are grouped and aligned to goals, required metrics, data results, input from educational partners, and address student
         groups and schools with the lowest performance levels on the CA School Dashboard indicators.

Goal Analysis [2024-25]
An analysis of how this goal was carried out in the previous year.
A description of overall implementation, including any substantive differences in planned actions and actual implementation of these actions,
and any relevant challenges and successes experienced with implementation.
No substantive differences to the actions as planned

Successes:
   • Expanded English Language Development staff growth to match growing need: action 1.21

2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                        Page 21 of 230
                                                                                                                                                     261
     •   Improved English language communication confirmed by educational partners: action 1.25
     •   Increased access to Dual Enrollment courses: action 1.06
     •   Bilingual Instructional Assistants (BIAs) provided language support in core courses: action 1.11

Challenges:
   • Hiring challenges impacted implementation: actions 1.10, 1.11, 1.21, 1.30, 1.32
   • Changes in student interests and needs: action 1.26




An explanation of material differences between Budgeted Expenditures and Estimated Actual Expenditures and/or Planned Percentages of
Improved Services and Estimated Actual Percentages of Improved Services.
Material differences from budgeted expenditures include actions 1.03, 1.06, 1.12, 1.14, 1.15, 1.18, 1.20, 1.22, 1.24, 1.26, 1.29, 1.30, 1.32,
1.37, and 1.39 for the following reasons:
    • Change in student needs
    • End of year and/or summer expenses
    • Hiring challenges
    • Increased staffing to support growing student enrollment
    • Multi-year action
    • Negotiated contract changes
    • Other funding available



A description of the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of the specific actions to date in making progress toward the goal.
English Language Arts (ELA) State Assessment: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate
decreased. Filipino and foster youth student groups had the largest increase. American Indian/Alaskan Native and White student groups had
the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.02, 1.09,
1.11, 1.12, 1.13, 1.14, 1.15, 1.17, 1.29, 1.30, and 1.32.

Mathematics State Assessment: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate increased. Filipino and
foster youth student groups had the largest increase. American Indian/Alaskan Native Asian, and long-term English learners student groups
had the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.02,
1.09, 1.11, 1.12, 1.14, 1.15, 1.17, 1.18, 1.19, and 1.41.

Science State Assessment: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. American
Indian/Alaskan native, foster youth, and homeless student groups had the largest increase. Asian and White student groups had the largest
decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.11, 1.12, 1.14,
1.15, and 1.37.

2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                  Page 22 of 231
                                                                                                                                               261
English Language Learner State Assessment: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased for both English learners and long-
term English learners. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.01,
1.20, 1.21, 1.22, 1.23, 1.24, 1.25, and 1.41.

English Language Learner Reclassification: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased. We have met our 3-year target
outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.01, 1.11, 1.12, 1.20, 1.21, 1.22, 1.23, 1.24, 1.25, 1.26, and 1.27.

College and Career Indicator (CCI Rate): Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate increased. Foster
youth, Hispanic, and homeless student groups had the largest increase. African American, Pacific Islander, and two or more races student
groups had the largest decrease. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include:
1.03,1.04, 1.05, 1.06, 1.07, 1.31, 1.33, 1.39, 1.40, and 1.41.

A-G Completion: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate increased. Filipino and homeless student
groups had the largest increase. American Indian/Alaskan Native, foster youth, Pacific Islander, and two or more races student groups had
the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.27 and
1.34.

CTE Completion: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. Foster youth student group
had the largest increase. Hispanic and students with disabilities student groups had the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year
target outcome. The LCAP action that contributed to this outcome include: 1.08

A-G and CTE Completion: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate increased. American
Indian/Alaskan Native student group had the largest increase. Filipino, foster youth, and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest
decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome.The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.08, 1.27, and 1.34.

College and Career Indicators (AP Exam Performance): Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate
increased. American Indian/Alaskan Native, Asian, Filipino, students with disabilities, and two or more races student groups had the largest
increase. Foster youth, homeless, and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest decrease. We have met our 3-year target outcome.
The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.16 and 1.38.

College and Career Indicators (IB Exam Performance): Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate
decreased. African American and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest increase. Filipino and homeless student groups had the
largest decrease. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 1.16 and 1.38.

College and Career Indicators (Early Assessment Program): Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate
increased. American Indian/Alaskan Native, Pacific Islander, and two or more races student groups had the largest increase. English learner
and students with disabilities student groups had the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions
that contributed to this outcome include: 1.02, 1.07, 1.09, 1.16, and 1.41.

on prior practice.
Previous action number 1.35 is now action 3.06 to align the action with Goal 3

Previous action number 2.13 is now action 1.13 to align the action with Goal 1

Actions 1.14, 1.24, and 1.36 have changed action title to better align with the intent of the action

Actions 1.16 and 1.32 have changed scope to better align with the intent of the action

Action 1.21 has changed LCFF priority student groups to better align with the intent of the action

Goals and Actions
Goal
  Goal #     Description                                                                                         Type of Goal
    2        Student Engagement and Course Access - Provide students with engaging programs, course work,        Broad Goal
             and opportunities that address attendance, dropout, graduation, and access to a broad course of
             study.
State Priorities addressed by this goal.
 XPriority 5: Pupil Engagement (Engagement)
 XPriority 7: Course Access (Conditions of Learning)

An explanation of why the LEA has developed this goal.
A review of Dashboard data results showed:
    • African American, English learner, and homeless student groups received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard
       English language arts and mathematics indicators.
    • Students with Disabilities student group received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard college/career indicator.
    • 29 out of 65 schools had one or more lowest performance levels on a 2023 Dashboard state indicator (see addendum A)
    • 53 out of 65 schools had one or more student groups that received the lowest performance level on a 2023 Dashboard state
       indicator (see addendum B).

During the 2021-24 LCAP cycle, educational partners indicated that:
    • LCAP goals were too broad and difficult to measure using data.
    • Including current year and previous year data in LCAP was confusing.
    • Not all required student groups were reported in the LCAP.

State and local data and educational partner input revealed a need to increase:
    • Alignment between goals, metrics, and actions.
    • Clarity to the public and board about our goals, and how we are measuring and monitoring progress.
    • Focus on English learner, low income, foster youth, and student groups with the lowest performance level on a Dashboard indicator.

As a result, the 2024-27 LCAP:
    • Goals are aligned to the state priority areas and required LCAP metrics.
    • Metrics include required state and local data that reflects the most recent full-year data as well as all education code required
       student groups in the reporting of data.
    • Actions are grouped and aligned to goals, required metrics, data results, input from educational partners, and address student
       groups and schools with the lowest performance levels on the CA School Dashboard indicators.

Goal Analysis [2024-25]
An analysis of how this goal was carried out in the previous year.
A description of overall implementation, including any substantive differences in planned actions and actual implementation of these actions,
and any relevant challenges and successes experienced with implementation.
No substantive differences to the actions as planned

Successes:
   • Immunization Clinic increased immunizations provided for school enrollment: action 2.01
   • McKinney-Vento (MKV) Transportation supported homeless attendance: action 2.18
   • Seat-based Summer School enrollment increased with language support provided: action 2.10

Challenges:
   • Hiring challenges impacted implementation: actions 2.05, 2.11, 2.17
   • Changes in student interests and needs: action 2.08



An explanation of material differences between Budgeted Expenditures and Estimated Actual Expenditures and/or Planned Percentages of
Improved Services and Estimated Actual Percentages of Improved Services.
Material differences from budgeted expenditures include actions 2.08, 2.09, 2.13, 2.16, 2.17, 2.18, and 2.19 for the following reasons:
   • Added staff to support the implementation of the action
   • End of year and/or summer expenses
   • Hiring challenges



A description of the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of the specific actions to date in making progress toward the goal.
Chronic Absenteeism: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate increased. American Indian/Alaskan
Native, students with disabilities, and White student groups had the largest decrease. Foster youth student group had the largest increase.
We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 2.01, 2.12, 2.15, 2.16, and 2.17.


2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                     Page 40 of 249
                                                                                                                                                  261
Attendance: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. Homeless, Pacific Islander, and
students with disabilities student groups had the largest decrease. Foster youth student group had the largest increase. We have met our 3-
year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 2.01, 2.02, 2.05, 2.12, 2.15, 2.16, and 2.17.

High School Dropout Rate: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. African American,
English learner, homeless, and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest decrease. American Indian/Alaskan Native and students with
disabilities student groups had the largest increase. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to
this outcome include: 2.27.

Middle School Dropout Rate: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The
LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 2.27 and 2.28.

Graduation Rate: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate increased. Filipino and Pacific Islander
student groups had the largest increase. Foster youth and students with disabilities student groups had the largest decrease. We have not
yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 2.02, 2.03, 2.04, 2.05, 2.06, 2.07, 2.08, 2.09,
2.10, 2.27, and 2.28.

Broad Course of Study: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate increased. American Indian/Alaskan
Native and Filipino student groups had the largest increase. Foster youth and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest decrease. We
have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 2.20, 2.21, 2.22, 2.23, 2.24, 2.25,
2.26, and 2.19.

Broad Course of Study: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. African American, foster
youth, and students with disabilities student groups had the largest increase. American Indian/Alaskan Native, English learner, and Filipino
student groups had the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome
include: 2.20, 2.21, 2.22, 2.23, 2.24, 2.25, 2.26, and 2.19.

Broad Course of Study: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate maintained. American
Indian/Alaskan Native and Pacific Islander student groups had the largest increase. Foster youth, homeless, and students with disabilities
student groups had the largest decrease. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome
include: 2.20, 2.21, 2.22, 2.23, 2.24, 2.25, 2.26, and 2.19.

Goal
  Goal #     Description                                                                                         Type of Goal
    3        School Conditions, Climate, and Family Engagement - Address factors both inside and outside the     Broad Goal
             classroom that impact student success such as family engagement, health, safety, discipline,
             connectedness, facilities, materials, and staffing.
State Priorities addressed by this goal.
 XPriority 1: Basic (Conditions of Learning)
 XPriority 3: Parental Involvement (Engagement)
 XPriority 6: School Climate (Engagement)

An explanation of why the LEA has developed this goal.
A review of Dashboard data results showed:
    • African American, English learner, and homeless student groups received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard
       English language arts and mathematics indicators.
    • Students with Disabilities student group received the lowest performance level on the 2023 Dashboard college/career indicator.
    • 29 out of 65 schools had one or more lowest performance levels on a 2023 Dashboard state indicator (see addendum A)
    • 53 out of 65 schools had one or more student groups that received the lowest performance level on a 2023 Dashboard state
       indicator (see addendum B).

During the 2021-24 LCAP cycle, educational partners indicated that:
    • LCAP goals were too broad and difficult to measure using data.
    • Including current year and previous year data in LCAP was confusing.
    • Not all required student groups were reported in the LCAP.

State and local data and educational partner input revealed a need to increase:
    • Alignment between goals, metrics, and actions.
    • Clarity to the public and board about our goals, and how we are measuring and monitoring progress.
    • Focus on English learner, low income, foster youth, and student groups with the lowest performance level on a Dashboard indicator.

As a result, the 2024-27 LCAP:
    • Goals are aligned to the state priority areas and required LCAP metrics.
    • Metrics include required state and local data that reflects the most recent full-year data as well as all education code required
       student groups in the reporting of data.
    • Actions are grouped and aligned to goals, required metrics, data results, input from educational partners, and address student
       groups and schools with the lowest performance levels on the CA School Dashboard indicators.

Goal Analysis [2024-25]
An analysis of how this goal was carried out in the previous year.
A description of overall implementation, including any substantive differences in planned actions and actual implementation of these actions,
and any relevant challenges and successes experienced with implementation.
Substantive differences:
   • Site Administrator Support was not implemented as written due to changing needs at the site level

Successes:
   • Summer Family Camps saw full enrollment and waitlists: action 3.09
   • Face Mobile & Family Outreach fully implemented Community Care Hubs: action 3.08
   • Camp Kinder participation among English learners provided school readiness: action 3.27

Challenges:
   • Hiring challenges impacted implementation: actions 3.15, 3.24, 3.32, 3.35, 3.60
   • Changes in student interests and needs: action 3.25, 3.26

An explanation of material differences between Budgeted Expenditures and Estimated Actual Expenditures and/or Planned Percentages of
Improved Services and Estimated Actual Percentages of Improved Services.
Material differences from budgeted expenditures include actions 3.05, 3.09, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, 3.14, 3.21, 3.23, 3.24, 3.27, 3.30, 3.35, 3.37,
3.38, 3.42, 3.50, 3.53, 3.60, 3.61 for the following reasons:
    • End of year and/or summer expenses
    • Hiring challenges
    • Increased staffing to support growing student enrollment
    • Negotiated contract changes
    • Other funding available
    • Reduced need at the site level



A description of the effectiveness or ineffectiveness of the specific actions to date in making progress toward the goal.
Suspension Rate: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased and the disparity rate decreased. Foster youth and two or more
races student groups had the largest decrease. African American and Filipino student groups had the largest increase. We have not yet met
our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.04, 3.11, 3.12, 3.13, 3.15, 3.16, 3.18, 3.19, 3.27,
3.33, 3.40, 3.41, and 3.63.

Expulsion Rate: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased and the disparity rate decreased. African American, foster youth,
and students with disabilities student groups had the largest decrease. American Indian/Alaskan Native and homeless student groups had
the largest increase. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.15 and 3.33.

Connectedness: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate maintained. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP
actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.02, 3.03, 3.04, 3.06, 3.07, 3.08, 3.09, 3.12, 3.13, 3.14, 3.15, 3.16, 3.17, 3.18, 3.20, 3.21,
3.22, 3.23, 3.24, 3.25, 3.26, 3.27, 3.28, 3.29, 3.30, 3.31, 3.32, 3.34, 3.35, 3.36, 3.37, 3.38, 3.39, 3.40, 3.41, 3.42, 3.44, 3.48, 3.54, 3.55, 3.56,
3.57, 3.58, 3.59, 3.60, and 3.61.

Safety: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate decreased. We have not yet met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP actions that
contributed to this outcome include: 3.02, 3.03, 3.04, 3.06, 3.13, 3.15, 3.17, 3.20, 3.21, 3.22, 3.23, 3.24, 3.26, 3.28, 3.29, 3.40, 3.41, 3.42,
3.44, 3.47, 3.54, 3.55, 3.57, and 3.58.

Parent and Family Engagement: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate increased. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The
LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.01, 3.05, 3.07, 3.08, 3.09, 3.10, 3.27, 3.35, 3.36, 3.37, 3.38, 3.39, 3.43, 3.45, 3.46,
3.48, 3.59, and 3.60.

School Facilities: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate was maintained. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP
actions that contributed to this outcome include: N/A


2025-26 Local Control and Accountability Plan for San Juan Unified School District                                                      Page 55 of 264
                                                                                                                                                   261
Standards Access: Between 2022-23 and 2023-24, the overall rate was maintained. We have met our 3-year target outcome. The LCAP
actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.32 and 3.52.

Credentialed Staffing: As of May 2025, the 2023-24 credentialed staffing data has not been released by the California Department of
Education in order to analyze and compare to 2022-23 data. The LCAP actions that contributed to this outcome include: 3.49, 3.50, 3.51,
3.53, 3.61, and 3.62.



A description of any changes made to the planned goal, metrics, target outcomes, or actions for the coming year that resulted from reflections
on prior practice.
Actions 3.02, 3.04, 3.08, 3.09, 3.35, 3.43, and 3.59 have changed action title to better align with the intent of the action

Actions 3.03 and 3.57 have changed scope to better align with the intent of the action

Actions 3.02, 3.18, 3.31, 3.35, 3.41, 3.42, 3.43, 3.45, 3.46, 3.57, and 3.59 have changed action description to better align with the intent of
the action

Action 3.12 has been eliminated and restructured into action 3.18 due to a reorganization of the MTSS program

Action 3.19 has been eliminated and restructured into action 3.17 due to a reorganization of the MTSS program

Action 3.32 has been eliminated and restructured into action 3.31 to consolidate inclusive practices

Action 3.06 has been eliminated to improve services offered through other actions

Previous action 3.60 is now action 3.11

Goals and Actions
Goal
  Goal #     Description                                                                                                 Type of Goal
    4        Schools with Non-Stability Rates of +25% and Socioeconomically Disadvantaged Rates of +70%                  Equity Multiplier Focus Goal
             (Starr King K-8) - Within three years, improve outcomes for student groups that have the lowest
             performance level on one or more state indicators, and address any underlying issues with
             credentialing, subject matter preparation, and retention of the school’s educators.
State Priorities addressed by this goal.
 XPriority 8: Other Pupil Outcomes (Pupil Outcomes)

An explanation of why the LEA has developed this goal.
Schools with socioeconomically disadvantaged rates of +70% and non-stability rates of +25% are eligible to receive state Equity Multiplier
Funds. (Non-Stability Rate = Percent of student who do not complete 180 instructional days at one school)

The State requirements to access these funds include developing at least one focus goal that addresses:
   • All pupil groups that have the lowest performance level on one or more state indicators on the California School Dashboard.
   • Any underlying issues in the credentialing, subject matter preparation, and retention of the school’s educators.

School sites receiving Equity Multiplier funds engaged their educational partners to identify the unique needs of the student groups with one
or more lowest performance levels on a 2024 Dashboard state indicator. Based on a review and analysis of educational partner input and
state/local data, Equity Multiplier schools developed LCAP actions that are principally directed to improving outcomes in the required areas.

and any relevant challenges and successes experienced with implementation.
No substantive differences to the actions as planned

   • Fieldtrips provided students with hands-on learning opportunities: action 13.04
   • Technology investments to support English Language Arts and math: actions 13.01, 13.02

Challenges:
   • Restorative practice training was not available: action 13.03
   • Hiring challenges affected action implementation: 13.03, 13.04
   • Supplies were purchased in lower amounts due to donations on hand: action 13.04

Chronic Absenteeism:
   • English learner and students with disabilities are no longer lowest performing on the 2024 Dashboard

Suspension: African American, Asian, Hispanic, socio-economically disadvantaged, two or more race, white
